questions.explanation: u podne = at noon; ujutro = in the morning; navečer = in the evening; u ponoć = at midnight.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: First I get up at seven o'clock.
questions.explanation: Sequencer first (Prvo), then verb, then time (u sedam sati).

questions.prompt: Radim ___ devet do pet. (from nine to five)
questions.explanation: od … do … = from … to …: Radim od devet do pet.

questions.prompt: Ujutro idem ___. (I go TO work)
questions.explanation: Motion = na + accusative (na posao). Location = na + locative (na poslu). Work takes na, not u.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: In the evening I have dinner and watch TV.
questions.explanation: Time word first, two verbs joined with i, accusative object (televizija → televiziju).

questions.prompt: What does 'ručam' mean?
questions.explanation: ručati = to have lunch (ručak = lunch); doručkovati = breakfast, večerati = dinner.

questions.prompt: Zatim ___ tuširam. (Then I shower — add the missing little word)
questions.explanation: tuširati se is reflexive; the clitic se stands in second position: Zatim se tuširam.

days.title: Shopping & quantities
days.objective: Buy things and talk about how much/many.
days.paretoFocus: Quantity words and shopping phrases are needed every time you shop or cook.

items: Ask for 'a little bread' and 'a lot of water'.

activities.title: Quantities & shopping phrases
activities.intro: Quantity words (malo, puno, kilogram, litra, pola…) are followed by the genitive: kruh → kruha, voda → vode, mlijeko → mlijeka. Don't worry — the genitive gets its own day later; today learn these as ready chunks, plus the fixed market phrases.

items.note: Quantity + genitive: kruh → kruha (m. adds -a).
voda → vode (f. -a → -e).
Genitive plural: jabuke → jabuka.
pola + genitive: pola kilograma, pola litre.
mlijeko → mlijeka (n. adds -a).
deka (decagrams) is how Croatians order at the counter: deset deka = 100 g.
Molim vas + what you want — the universal polite request.
As a question, Izvolite? = 'What can I get you?' — the standard seller's opener.

items.en: How much does it cost? / How much do the apples cost?
items.note: Singular subject → košta; plural subject → koštaju.

items.hr: Želite li još nešto? — Ne, hvala, to je sve.
items.en: Would you like anything else? — No thanks, that's all.
items.note: Your ready-made exit from any shop conversation.
The genitive here means 'some/any' — a very Croatian touch.

activities.title: Shopping & quantities drill
activities.intro: Quantity + genitive chunks and the market phrases you'll need in the dialogue.

questions.prompt: malo ___ (kruh — a little bread)
questions.explanation: Quantity words take the genitive: masculine nouns add -a (kruh → kruha).

questions.prompt: puno ___ (voda — a lot of water)
questions.explanation: Feminine -a → -e in the genitive: voda → vode. (vodu is the accusative: Pijem vodu.)

questions.prompt: The seller says 'Izvolite?' — what do they mean?
questions.explanation: Izvolite? opens the exchange; Izvolite. (statement) = 'Here you are' when handing something over.

questions.prompt: Build the polite request: A kilo of apples, please.
questions.explanation: Molim vas + quantity + genitive plural (jabuke → jabuka).

questions.prompt: litra ___ (mlijeko — a litre of milk)
questions.explanation: Neuter nouns add -a in the genitive: mlijeko → mlijeka.

questions.prompt: 'Želite li još nešto?' — the polite way to say no:
questions.explanation: 'No thanks, that's all.' — the standard closing line at any counter.

questions.prompt: Why is it 'Koliko koštaju jabuke?' but 'Koliko košta sir?'
options: jabuke is plural, sir is singular
jabuke is feminine, sir is masculine
questions.answer: jabuke is plural, sir is singular
questions.explanation: The verb agrees with the subject: jabuke koštaju (they cost), sir košta (it costs).

questions.prompt: pola kilograma ___ (sir — half a kilo of cheese)
questions.explanation: Another quantity chunk: pola kilograma + genitive (sir → sira).

questions.prompt: 'Sve zajedno četiri eura i pedeset centi.' — how much do you pay?
questions.explanation: četiri = 4, pedeset = 50: four euros and fifty cents. sve zajedno = all together.

activities.title: At the market (Na tržnici)
activities.intro: A full shopping scene at a Zagreb market stall. Act out both roles, then re-run it swapping in different foods and quantities. Notice every quantity is followed by the genitive.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Izvolite?
lines.en: Good day! What can I get you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Molim vas kilogram jabuka.
lines.en: Good day! A kilo of apples, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Izvolite. Želite li još nešto?
lines.en: Here you are. Would you like anything else?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da, molim pola kilograma sira i dvadeset deka šunke.
lines.en: Yes, half a kilo of cheese and 200 grams of ham, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Imamo i svjež kruh.
lines.en: We also have fresh bread.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Super! Jedan kruh, molim. I malo rajčica.
lines.en: Great! One loaf, please. And a few tomatoes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Koliko rajčica želite?
lines.en: How many tomatoes would you like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Pola kilograma, molim. Koliko košta sve zajedno?
lines.en: Half a kilo, please. How much is everything together?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sve zajedno devet eura i dvadeset centi.
lines.en: All together nine euros and twenty cents.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Izvolite deset eura.
lines.en: Here's ten euros.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hvala! Izvolite ostatak. Doviđenja!
lines.en: Thank you! Here's your change. Goodbye!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala lijepa! Doviđenja!
lines.en: Thank you very much! Goodbye!

days.title: Connectors & longer sentences
days.objective: Join ideas with i, ali, jer, pa, onda.
days.paretoFocus: Connectors turn isolated phrases into real discourse — the jump from words to conversation.

activities.title: Eight connectors: from phrases to real sentences
activities.intro: Connectors are the glue that turns two short sentences into one real thought. Croatian's core eight cover almost everything: i, a, ali, ili, jer, pa, onda, zato. Note the subtle one: a sits between i (and) and ali (but) — a mild contrast.

items.en: and — I drink coffee and tea.
items.note: Joins two alike things.

items.hr: a — Ja radim, a ti učiš.
items.en: and/whereas — I work, and (whereas) you study.
items.note: Mild contrast: stronger than i, weaker than ali. Very common.

items.en: but — I'm tired, but I'm working.
items.note: Direct opposition.

items.hr: ili — Želiš li kavu ili čaj?
items.en: or — Do you want coffee or tea?
items.note: Also ili … ili … = either … or …

items.hr: jer — Učim hrvatski jer živim u Zagrebu.
items.en: because — I'm learning Croatian because I live in Zagreb.
items.note: Never start an answer to 'Zašto?' without it: Zašto? — Jer…

items.hr: pa — Prvo radim, pa idem kući.
items.en: so/and then — First I work, and then I go home.
items.note: Loose, conversational 'so/then'.

items.hr: onda — Doručkujem, onda idem na posao.
items.en: then — I have breakfast, then I go to work.
items.note: You know it from sequencing; it also means 'in that case': Onda dobro!

items.hr: zato — Pada kiša, zato sam kod kuće.
items.en: that's why — It's raining, that's why I'm at home.
items.note: zato = that's why; zato što = because (synonym of jer).

items.hr: Ne govorim dobro, ali učim svaki dan jer želim živjeti u Hrvatskoj.
items.en: I don't speak well, but I study every day because I want to live in Croatia.
items.note: Three clauses, two connectors — this is what 'longer sentences' means.

activities.title: Connector drill: join the ideas
activities.intro: Pick the connector that fits the logic: addition, contrast, choice, reason or sequence.

questions.prompt: Umoran sam, ___ radim. (I'm tired, BUT I'm working)
questions.explanation: Direct opposition = ali (but).

questions.prompt: Učim hrvatski ___ živim u Zagrebu. (because)
questions.explanation: jer = because. zato što also works: Učim hrvatski zato što živim u Zagrebu.

questions.prompt: Želiš li kavu ___ čaj?
questions.explanation: A choice between two options = ili (or).

questions.prompt: Ja radim, ___ ti učiš. (mild contrast: I work, whereas you study)
questions.explanation: a marks mild contrast between two parallel statements — between i and ali in strength.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: I'm learning Croatian because I live in Zagreb.
questions.answer: Učim hrvatski jer živim u Zagrebu
questions.explanation: Main clause + jer + reason clause. u Zagrebu = locative (location).

questions.prompt: Pada kiša, ___ sam kod kuće. (that's why)
questions.explanation: zato = that's why (result). jer would give the reason: Kod kuće sam jer pada kiša.

questions.prompt: Prvo radim, ___ idem kući. (so/and then — the loose conversational one)
questions.explanation: pa = so/and then. onda is also accepted — both sequence the two actions.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: I don't speak well, but I'm learning.
questions.explanation: ne + verb for negation, ali for the contrast. (In writing there'd be a comma before ali.)

questions.prompt: Pijem kavu ___ čaj. (simple addition: coffee AND tea)
questions.explanation: Plain addition of two alike things = i.

days.title: Listening day (comprehensible input)
days.objective: Train your ear with clear Croatian.
days.paretoFocus: Listening is half of communication and the most-neglected skill. Regular input accelerates everything else.
drills: Replay the audio from your two most recent A1.hr units, twice each.

items: Note 5 new words and re-test them tomorrow.

activities.title: Phrases that save a conversation
activities.intro: Listening day. Before training your ear, arm yourself with the repair kit — the phrases that keep a conversation alive when you don't understand. These are among the most valuable sentences you will ever learn.

items.note: With rising intonation — the quickest way to ask for a repeat.
Formal (vi). To a friend: Možeš li ponoviti?
Your best friend in week one in Croatia.
razumjeti → razumijem. Say it early and often.
Signal you're following — keeps the other person talking.
značiti = to mean. Point at any word and ask.

items.en: How do you say "apple" in Croatian?
items.note: The reverse question — mines new vocabulary from any conversation.
Croatian is phonetic: once you see it written, you can say it.

activities.title: Ear training: listen and answer
activities.intro: Most questions here are audio-only: the sentence is spoken aloud and hidden. Listen, then answer. Croatian spelling is phonetic — if you can hear it, you can write it.

questions.prompt: Listen: where and when is this person going?
questions.explanation: Idem na more u subotu. = I'm going to the seaside on Saturday. (na more = motion; u subotu = on Saturday)

questions.prompt: Type the word you hear.
questions.explanation: hvala = thank you. The h is a light 'kh' sound.

questions.prompt: Type the word you hear. (Careful with the first letter!)
questions.explanation: škola = school. š = the 'sh' sound — and it must be written with the haček.

questions.prompt: Type the word you hear. (A common goodbye.)
questions.explanation: doviđenja = goodbye. The đ is the soft 'j' of 'jeans' — one letter, not 'dj'.

questions.prompt: Listen: where does Ana work?
questions.explanation: Ana radi u banci, a živi u Zagrebu. — She WORKS in a bank (u banci) and LIVES in Zagreb. Don't confuse radi and živi.
questions.audioText: Ana radi u banci, a živi u Zagrebu.

questions.prompt: The word 'kuća' (house) contains which letter?
questions.explanation: kuća has the soft ć. Minimal pairs like č/ć are the hardest sounds to hear — listen for the softer, more palatal ć.

questions.prompt: Type the word you hear. (It means 'woman'.)
questions.explanation: žena = woman. ž = the 's' in 'pleasure', written with a haček.

questions.prompt: Shadow the repair phrase: Can you repeat, please?
questions.explanation: Verb + li makes the question: Možete li ponoviti? Then the politeness tag molim vas.

questions.prompt: Listen: when is this person going to the cinema?
questions.explanation: Sutra idem u kino. = Tomorrow I'm going to the cinema.

days.title: The past tense (perfekt) — intro
days.objective: Form the everyday past with biti + past participle.
days.paretoFocus: The perfekt is THE past tense of speech. With it you can tell stories about anything.
drills: Form the past of raditi: radio/radila sam.

items: Say 'I worked' as a man and as a woman.

activities.title: The perfekt: biti + participle
activities.intro: The everyday past tense of spoken Croatian = the short present of biti (sam, si, je…) + the past participle. The participle is easy: replace the infinitive -ti with -o (m), -la (f), -lo (n). A handful of key verbs are irregular — learn them as words.

items.note: The rule: cut -ti, add -o/-la/-lo. radi-ti → radi-o.
gleda-ti → gleda-o / gleda-la.
Irregular: the s drops (not 'jesao').
Irregular. Jučer sam bio u gradu. = Yesterday I was in town.
Irregular: ići → išao (m) / išla (f). The most useful irregular of all.
The participle shows YOUR gender — men say radio, women say radila.
sam is a clitic: it must stand in second position, right after the first word.

activities.title: Perfekt word order, questions and negation
activities.intro: The auxiliary (sam, si, je…) is a clitic — it can never stand first. Questions use je + li → 'Jesi li…?', and the negative forms (nisam, nisi…) are stressed, so they CAN stand first.

items.note: First word (Jučer), then the clitic sam, then the participle.
Participle first also works — sam still sits in second position.
Questions use the LONG form + li: Jesi li…? Jeste li…?

items.en: What did you do yesterday? (to a woman)
items.note: After a question word, the clitic si comes immediately: Što si…?
Negative biti + participle. Nisam is stressed, so it may stand first.

items.hr: Gdje si bio? — Bio sam u gradu.
items.en: Where were you? — I was in town.
items.note: Perfekt + locative: yesterday's grammar working together.

activities.title: First steps in the past
activities.intro: Form participles, place the clitic correctly, and build your first past-tense sentences.

questions.prompt: The past participle of raditi for a male speaker is:
questions.explanation: Cut -ti, add -o for masculine: raditi → radio. (radila = f, radilo = n)

questions.prompt: Jučer ___ učio hrvatski. (I studied — add the auxiliary)
questions.explanation: Perfekt = biti + participle. For ja the auxiliary is sam, in second position: Jučer sam učio.

questions.prompt: The participle of ići for a female speaker is:
questions.explanation: ići is irregular: išao (m), išla (f), išlo (n). Išla sam u grad. = I went to town (woman).

questions.prompt: Sinoć sam ___ film. (gledati — female speaker: Last night I watched a film)
questions.explanation: gledati → gledala for a female speaker: Sinoć sam gledala film.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: Yesterday I worked at home. (male speaker)
questions.explanation: Jučer first, the clitic sam second, then the participle. kod kuće = at home.

questions.prompt: 'Gdje si bio jučer?' — which reply is correct?
questions.explanation: The clitic sam can't stand first — 'Sam bio…' is impossible. Bio sam u gradu puts it correctly in second position.

questions.prompt: Jutros sam ___ kavu. (piti — male speaker: This morning I drank coffee)
questions.explanation: piti → pio (m) / pila (f): Jutros sam pio kavu.

questions.prompt: The participle of jesti (to eat) for a male speaker is:
questions.explanation: jesti is irregular — the s drops: jeo (m), jela (f). Jeo sam sendvič.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: Last night I watched television. (female speaker)
questions.explanation: Sinoć (last night) first, clitic sam second, female participle gledala, accusative object televiziju.

days.title: Perfekt: gender agreement
days.objective: Make the past participle agree with the subject.
days.paretoFocus: The participle changes with gender/number — a uniquely Slavic feature worth drilling so your past tense sounds right.
drills: Convert 5 sentences to female and plural speakers.

items: Spot a wrong-gender participle and fix it.

activities.title: Participle agreement: -o, -la, -lo, -li, -le, -la
activities.intro: The participle agrees with the SUBJECT in gender and number — a signature Slavic feature. Singular: -o (m), -la (f), -lo (n). Plural: -li (m or mixed group), -le (all-female), -la (neuter). Plus: negation with nisam, and the one quirk of reflexive verbs.

items.note: Your own gender decides: men -o, women -la.
Neuter subject → -lo.

items.en: We worked. (men or a mixed group)
items.note: Any group with at least one male → -li.
Only when everyone is female → -le.
Neuter plural → -la: sela su bila.

items.hr: Nisam radio. / Nisi radila. / Nije radilo.
items.en: I didn't work. / You didn't work. / It didn't work.
items.note: Negative = nisam/nisi/nije… + participle. Nisam can stand first.
Reflexive quirk: with se, the 3rd person singular drops je — never 'se je'.

items.hr: Marko i Ana su išli u kino.
items.en: Marko and Ana went to the cinema.
items.note: Mixed pair → -li.

items.hr: Ana i Iva su išle na more.
items.en: Ana and Iva went to the seaside.
items.note: Two women → -le.

activities.title: Gender & number agreement drill
activities.intro: Convert participles between genders and numbers, negate, and catch the reflexive trap.

questions.prompt: Ana i Marija su ___ u školu. (ići)
questions.explanation: An all-female group takes -le: Ana i Marija su išle.

questions.prompt: A woman says 'I worked yesterday': ___ sam jučer. (raditi)
questions.explanation: Female speaker → -la: Radila sam jučer.

questions.prompt: Marko i Ana su ___ cijeli dan. (raditi — a mixed pair)
questions.explanation: Any group containing a male takes -li, even one man among many women.

questions.prompt: How does a man say 'I didn't work'?
questions.explanation: ne + sam fuse into nisam; male speaker keeps -o: Nisam radio.

questions.prompt: Mi (grupa muškaraca — a group of men) smo ___ hrvatski. (učiti)
questions.explanation: Masculine plural → -li: Učili smo hrvatski.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct? (He laughed.)
questions.explanation: With reflexive se, the 3rd person singular auxiliary je is dropped: On se smijao — never 'se je'.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: Ana and Iva went to school.
questions.answer: Ana i Iva su išle u školu
questions.explanation: All-female subject → išle. u školu = motion (accusative), not u školi.

questions.prompt: Dijete je ___. (spavati — The child slept)
questions.explanation: Neuter singular subject → -lo: Dijete je spavalo.

questions.prompt: 'Gdje ste bili?' asked to a group of women — the correct answer:
questions.explanation: All-female 'we' → bile: Bile smo na moru. (Men or a mixed group would say bili.)

days.title: Phase 1 milestone: a real conversation
days.objective: Hold a simple but real conversation about yourself and the past.
days.paretoFocus: Integrating present and past in live speech is the true A1 milestone — proof the core works together.
drills: Cover: who you are, your routine, yesterday.

items: Take the A1 quiz in the app.

activities.title: Your milestone script: the building blocks
activities.intro: Today you prove Phase 1: a real conversation covering (1) who you are, (2) your routine, (3) yesterday — in the perfekt. These nine frames are the skeleton; fill them with your own life, say each aloud, then run the dialogue below.

items.note: Your opener since day 3 — now it starts a real conversation.

items.hr: Imam trideset godina i živim u Zagrebu.
items.en: I am thirty years old and I live in Zagreb.
items.note: Age with imati + locative for where you live.

items.en: I've been learning Croatian for thirty days now.
items.note: već + time = 'for … already'. Croatian uses the present here.

items.hr: Svaki dan prvo ustajem, onda doručkujem i idem na posao.
items.en: Every day I first get up, then have breakfast and go to work.
items.note: Your day-24 routine chain, compressed to one sentence.

items.en: I work from nine to five.

items.hr: Jučer sam ustao u osam. / Jučer sam ustala u osam.
items.en: Yesterday I got up at eight. (m / f)
items.note: Routine verbs flip into the perfekt: ustati → ustao/ustala.

items.hr: Išao sam u grad i kupio sam kruh. / Išla sam u grad i kupila sam kruh.
items.en: I went to town and bought bread. (m / f)
items.note: ići → išao/išla; kupiti → kupio/kupila. Keep the participles agreeing with YOU.

items.hr: Navečer sam bio umoran. / Navečer sam bila umorna.
items.en: In the evening I was tired. (m / f)
items.note: Adjective agrees too: umoran (m) / umorna (f).

items.hr: Bilo je teško, ali sada mogu razgovarati na hrvatskom.
items.en: It was hard, but now I can have a conversation in Croatian.
items.note: Your closing line — and it's true.

activities.title: Phase 1 milestone check
activities.intro: Ten questions across everything from days 21-29: locative, days and months, routine, quantities, connectors and the perfekt. This is your A1 gate.

questions.prompt: 'I am in the city' (location, not motion):
questions.explanation: Static location = u + locative: u gradu. (u grad = accusative, motion: Idem u grad.)

questions.prompt: Jučer ___ kupila kruh. (a woman: Yesterday I bought bread)
questions.explanation: Perfekt auxiliary for ja = sam, in second position: Jučer sam kupila kruh.

questions.prompt: Which day comes after petak?
questions.explanation: petak (Friday) → subota (Saturday).

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: Yesterday I went to the market. (female speaker)
questions.explanation: Jučer + clitic sam (second position) + išla (f) + na tržnicu (motion, accusative).

questions.prompt: malo ___ (kruh — a little bread)
questions.explanation: Quantity + genitive: kruh → kruha.

questions.prompt: Prvo doručkujem, ___ idem na posao, na kraju spavam.
questions.explanation: The sequence chain: prvo → onda → na kraju.

questions.prompt: 'Što ste radili jučer?' — the best answer (male speaker):
options: Učio sam hrvatski i gledao sam film.
Učila sam hrvatski i gledala sam film.
questions.answer: Učio sam hrvatski i gledao sam film.
questions.explanation: Past question → perfekt answer, male participles (-o), and the clitic sam never first. The present-tense option answers a different question.

questions.prompt: Jučer sam ___ u Zagrebu. (biti — female speaker: Yesterday I was in Zagreb)
questions.explanation: biti → bio (m) / bila (f): Jučer sam bila u Zagrebu.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: I work from nine to five.
questions.explanation: od … do … = from … to …: Radim od devet do pet.

questions.prompt: 'u kolovozu' means:
questions.explanation: kolovoz = August (a native Croatian month name); u kolovozu = in August (locative).

activities.title: The milestone conversation: who I am, my day, yesterday
activities.intro: The Phase 1 milestone, as a full script: introduce yourself, describe your routine, and tell what you did yesterday — in the perfekt. Your lines use masculine participles (radio/išao); women say radila/išla.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Drago mi je. Recite mi nešto o sebi — tko ste vi?
lines.en: Good day! Nice to meet you. Tell me something about yourself — who are you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Zovem se Ana. Imam trideset godina i živim u Zagrebu. Učim hrvatski već trideset dana.
lines.en: My name is Ana. I'm thirty years old and I live in Zagreb. I've been learning Croatian for thirty days now.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlično! Kakav je vaš dan? Što radite svaki dan?
lines.en: Excellent! What is your day like? What do you do every day?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Prvo ustajem u sedam, onda doručkujem i pijem kavu. Zatim idem na posao.
lines.en: First I get up at seven, then I have breakfast and drink coffee. Then I go to work.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A poslije posla?
lines.en: And after work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Poslije posla idem u trgovinu. Navečer večeram, učim hrvatski i gledam televiziju.
lines.en: After work I go to the shop. In the evening I have dinner, study Croatian and watch TV.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super. A što ste radili jučer?
lines.en: Great. And what did you do yesterday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jučer sam ustao u osam. Doručkovao sam i išao sam u grad.
lines.en: Yesterday I got up at eight. I had breakfast and went to town.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Što ste kupili?
lines.en: What did you buy?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kupio sam kruh, sir i kilogram jabuka na tržnici.
lines.en: I bought bread, cheese and a kilo of apples at the market.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Jeste li učili hrvatski jučer?
lines.en: Did you study Croatian yesterday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jesam! Učio sam jedan sat i slušao sam radio. Navečer sam bio umoran i spavao sam.
lines.en: I did! I studied for an hour and listened to the radio. In the evening I was tired and I slept.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! Sada govorite hrvatski. Čestitam!
lines.en: Bravo! You speak Croatian now. Congratulations!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala vam! Bilo je teško, ali sada mogu razgovarati na hrvatskom.
lines.en: Thank you! It was hard, but now I can have a conversation in Croatian.

days.title: The future tense (futur)
days.objective: Talk about the future with htjeti (short) + infinitive.
days.paretoFocus: The futur is regular and reuses infinitives you know; it completes your past-present-future toolkit.
drills: Learn ću, ćeš, će, ćemo, ćete, će.

activities.title: Futur I — ću, ćeš, će + infinitive
activities.intro: The future tense is the easiest tense in Croatian: the short present of htjeti (ću, ćeš, će, ćemo, ćete, će) plus an infinitive you already know. The only trick is spelling — it depends on what comes first in the sentence.

items.hr: ću, ćeš, će, ćemo, ćete, će
items.en: the future helper — short present of htjeti
items.note: Unstressed clitics: they can never stand first in a neutral sentence.
Infinitive first: raditi drops its final -i → radit + ću, pronounced as one word but written as two.
Same rule for every -ti verb: gledati → gledat before the clitic.
Verbs in -ći keep the full infinitive: doći ću, ići ćemo.
If anything else starts the sentence, the clitic comes second and the infinitive keeps its full -ti form.
After a pronoun the clitic is in second position and the infinitive stays whole.

items.hr: neću, nećeš, neće, nećemo, nećete, neće
items.note: Stressed forms written as one word — they MAY start the sentence: Neću raditi u nedjelju.
Questions use the stressed forms + li: Hoćeš li...? Hoćete li...?

items.en: See you tomorrow (we will see each other).
items.note: With reflexive verbs, se joins the clitic cluster: vidjet ćemo se.

activities.title: Future forms & radit ću spelling
activities.intro: Drill the clitics, the negative forms and the word-order spelling rule.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct future helper for 'mi': Sutra ___ ići na more.
questions.explanation: mi → ćemo: Sutra ćemo ići na more.

questions.prompt: Complete: Radit ___ sutra. (I will work tomorrow)
questions.explanation: ja → ću. Infinitive first, clitic second: Radit ću sutra.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is spelled correctly?
questions.explanation: When the infinitive comes first, -ti verbs drop the final -i, and clitic and verb are written as two separate words: Radit ću.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: ___ ću u osam sati.
questions.explanation: Verbs in -ći keep the FULL infinitive before the clitic: Doći ću u osam sati.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of učiti: Sutra ću ___ hrvatski.
questions.explanation: Because 'Sutra' starts the sentence, the clitic ću is already in second position — the infinitive keeps its full form: Sutra ću učiti.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'Tomorrow I will buy a train ticket.'
questions.answer: Sutra ću kupiti kartu za vlak.
questions.explanation: The clitic ću stands in second position after Sutra; the infinitive stays whole: Sutra ću kupiti kartu za vlak.

questions.prompt: Say 'I will not work on Sunday': ___ raditi u nedjelju.
questions.explanation: The negative future uses the stressed one-word forms neću, nećeš... which may begin the sentence: Neću raditi u nedjelju.

questions.prompt: How do you ask a friend 'Will you come?'
questions.explanation: Questions use the stressed form + li: Hoćeš li doći? The unstressed ćeš can never start a question, and hoće li is 3rd person.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'We will see each other next week.'
questions.explanation: Infinitive first (vidjeti → vidjet), then the clitic cluster ćemo se: Vidjet ćemo se sljedeći tjedan.

days.title: Verb aspect — the big idea
days.objective: Understand imperfective vs perfective verb pairs.
days.paretoFocus: Aspect replaces a lot of English tense machinery. Grasping the concept now saves confusion later.
drills: Decide ongoing vs completed for 5 actions.
Note prefixes often make a verb perfective.

items: Pick the right aspect for 3 examples.

activities.title: Verb aspect — the big idea
activities.intro: Almost every Croatian verb comes in a pair: an imperfective verb for ongoing, repeated or unfinished actions, and a perfective verb for one whole completed event. English hides this in tenses ('was writing' vs 'wrote'); Croatian builds it into the verb itself.

items.en: to drink (process/habit) → to drink up (one completed act)
items.note: The prefix po- makes the verb perfective.

items.en: to be writing → to write (and finish)
items.note: Prefix na-.

items.en: to be reading → to read through (to the end)
items.note: Prefix pro-.

items.en: to be watching → to take a look
items.note: Prefixes very often make a verb perfective: po-, na-, pro-, u-...
učiti = the process, naučiti = the result.
Not all pairs use a prefix — some change the stem.
Habit or repeated action → imperfective piti.

items.en: I drank up my coffee and left.
items.note: One finished event → perfective popiti.

items.en: She was writing the letter all morning.
items.note: Duration, no statement about finishing → imperfective.

items.hr: Napisala je pismo i poslala ga.
items.en: She wrote the letter and sent it.
items.note: Completed, result matters → perfective.
Perfective verbs cannot describe what is happening right now — for 'I am writing' you must use the imperfective.

activities.title: Ongoing or completed?
activities.intro: Choose the right member of each aspect pair.

questions.prompt: Which aspect describes an ongoing, repeated or unfinished action?
questions.explanation: Imperfective = process, habit, duration. Perfective = one whole completed event.

questions.prompt: Svaki dan ___ novine.
questions.explanation: 'Svaki dan' signals a habit → imperfective čitati: Svaki dan čitam novine.

questions.prompt: Jučer sam ___ cijelu knjigu.
questions.explanation: One completed event ('the whole book, yesterday') → perfective pročitati in the perfekt: Jučer sam pročitao cijelu knjigu.

questions.prompt: Complete with the perfective partner of pisati (f. past): Ana je ___ pismo i poslala ga.
questions.explanation: The letter was finished and sent → perfective napisati: Ana je napisala pismo i poslala ga.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means 'I am writing an email' (right now)?
questions.explanation: Perfective verbs have no true present meaning — for an action happening now you need imperfective pisati: Pišem mail.

questions.prompt: Complete with the perfective partner of piti (m. past): ___ sam sok i otišao.
questions.explanation: One finished act of drinking → popiti: Popio sam sok i otišao.

questions.prompt: What is the perfective partner of kupovati?
questions.explanation: kupovati (buy regularly) / kupiti (buy once) — a pair that changes the stem instead of adding a prefix.

questions.prompt: Dugo sam učio hrvatski i napokon sam ___ padeže.
questions.explanation: napokon (finally) points to a completed result → perfective naučiti: napokon sam naučio padeže.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'I drank up my coffee and went to work.'
questions.answer: Popio sam kavu i otišao na posao.
questions.explanation: Two completed events in a row → perfective verbs: Popio sam kavu i otišao na posao.

days.title: Genitive case — intro
days.objective: Use the genitive for possession, 'of', and after most numbers.
days.paretoFocus: Genitive is the most-used case after nom/acc — possession, quantities and many prepositions all need it.

activities.title: Genitive — čaša vode, nema kruha
activities.intro: The genitive is the 'of' case: possession, quantities and many prepositions. Singular endings: feminine -a → -e, masculine adds -a, neuter -o/-e → -a. It is the most-used case after nominative and accusative.

items.note: voda → vode: feminine -a → -e.
kava → kave.
vino → vina: neuter -o → -a.
kruh → kruha: masculine adds -a.
After nema (there isn't) the noun is ALWAYS genitive.
The most famous nema-phrase of all.

items.en: little time, a lot of work
items.note: Quantity words (malo, puno, dosta, nekoliko) take the genitive.

items.hr: iz, od, do, kod, bez, blizu, pored
items.en: from, from, to, at (someone's), without, near, next to
items.note: These prepositions all take the genitive: iz Zagreba, bez šećera, kod prijatelja.
Possession = genitive: sestra → sestre.

activities.title: Genitive transformations
activities.intro: Turn nouns into the genitive after quantities, nema and prepositions.

questions.prompt: čaša ___ (a glass of water)
questions.explanation: voda → vode: feminine -a → -e in the genitive singular. čaša vode = a glass of water.

questions.prompt: Complete with the genitive of kava: šalica ___
questions.explanation: kava → kave: feminine nouns swap -a for -e. šalica kave = a cup of coffee.

questions.prompt: Complete with the genitive of kruh: Nema ___.
questions.explanation: After nema the noun must be genitive; masculine nouns add -a: Nema kruha.

questions.prompt: Which ending does a feminine -a noun take in the genitive singular?
questions.explanation: Feminine -a → -e (kava → kave). -u is the accusative, -i the dative/locative, -om the instrumental.

questions.prompt: Pijem kavu bez ___.
questions.explanation: bez (without) takes the genitive; masculine šećer adds -a: bez šećera.

questions.prompt: Complete with the genitive: Ja sam iz ___. (Zagreb)
questions.explanation: iz (from) takes the genitive: iz Zagreba, iz Splita, iz Amerike.

questions.prompt: U hladnjaku nema ___.
questions.explanation: nema + genitive; neuter mlijeko → mlijeka (-o → -a): U hladnjaku nema mlijeka.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'One glass of water, please.'
questions.explanation: čašu is accusative (the thing you ask for), vode is genitive ('of water'): Molim vas jednu čašu vode.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'my sister's book'?
questions.explanation: Possession uses the genitive of the possessor: moja sestra → moje sestre. knjiga moje sestre = my sister's book.

days.title: Dative & locative refresher
days.objective: Use the dative (to/for) and revisit the locative (location).
days.paretoFocus: Dative and locative often share endings; learning them together is efficient and covers 'to me' and 'in the city'.
drills: Contrast dative (motion to) vs locative (static in).

items: Say 'I'm giving it to my friend'.

activities.title: Dative & locative — to someone, in somewhere
activities.intro: The dative marks the person you give, say or send something TO. The locative uses the very same endings but ALWAYS follows a preposition (u, na, o) for static location or a topic. Learn them together — two cases for the price of one.

items.note: Masculine and neuter dative/locative singular: -u.

items.en: to a woman / to a sister
items.note: Feminine: -a → -i.

items.hr: ruka → ruci, knjiga → knjizi, noga → nozi
items.note: Sibilarization before -i: k → c, g → z (ruka → ruci, knjiga → knjizi).

items.en: I am giving my friend a book.
items.note: Recipient = dative (prijatelju), direct object = accusative (knjigu).

items.hr: pomagati, vjerovati, telefonirati, čestitati, zahvaliti + dativ
items.en: to help, believe, phone, congratulate, thank someone
items.note: These verbs govern the dative directly: Pomažem majci. Vjerujem ti. Telefoniram bratu.

items.hr: u gradu, na poslu, u školi
items.en: in the city, at work, at school
items.note: Locative = same endings, but always with a preposition: u/na for place.

items.hr: Pišem baki pismo. / Pričamo o baki.
items.en: I'm writing grandma a letter. / We're talking about grandma.
items.note: Dative = to/for someone (no preposition needed); locative = after u/na/o for place or topic.

activities.title: Dative recipients & locative places
activities.intro: Give things to people and say where things happen.

questions.prompt: Complete with the dative of prijatelj: Dajem ___ knjigu.
questions.explanation: The recipient takes the dative; masculine nouns add -u: Dajem prijatelju knjigu.

questions.prompt: Pomažem ___. (I help my mother)
questions.explanation: pomagati governs the dative; majka → majci (k → c before -i).

questions.prompt: Complete with the locative of knjiga: Sve piše u ___.
questions.explanation: u + locative; knjiga → knjizi with sibilarization g → z.

questions.prompt: Telefoniram ___. (I'm phoning my brother)
questions.explanation: telefonirati takes the dative: brat → bratu.

questions.prompt: Which case follows u/na when you say where something IS?
questions.explanation: Static location uses u/na + locative: u gradu, na poslu. (u/na + accusative would mean motion INTO/ONTO.)

questions.prompt: Complete with the locative: Živim u ___. (Zagreb)
questions.explanation: u + locative for where you live; masculine adds -u: Živim u Zagrebu.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'I am writing grandma a long letter.'
questions.explanation: baki = dative recipient (baka → baki), dugo pismo = accusative direct object.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence:
questions.explanation: o (about) takes the locative: posao → poslu. Razgovaramo o poslu = we're talking about work.

questions.prompt: Complete with the dative of sestra: Čestitam ___ rođendan!
questions.explanation: čestitati governs the dative: sestra → sestri. Čestitam sestri rođendan!

days.title: Describing people & places
days.objective: Use adjectives + cases to describe in detail.
days.paretoFocus: Rich description is a core A1/A2 skill and forces adjective agreement across cases.

activities.title: Describing people & places
activities.intro: Rich description = adjectives + agreement. Looks use imati + accusative (Ima plave oči); personality and places use biti + nominative (Ona je duhovita, Grad je bučan).

items.note: f.: visoka / niska.
debeo is blunt — punašan (chubby) is kinder.
imati + accusative; the adjectives agree: duga smeđa kosa → dugu smeđu kosu.

items.hr: Ima plave oči i nosi naočale.
items.en: He/She has blue eyes and wears glasses.
items.note: oči is feminine plural → plave oči.
Feminine: ljubazna, duhovita, pametna, vesela, ozbiljna.
Also for places: lijep grad, ružna zgrada.

items.en: The city is big and noisy.
items.note: Places: biti + nominative adjectives.

items.hr: Moj stan je mali, ali svijetao.
items.en: My flat is small but bright.
items.note: svijetao (m.), svijetla (f.), svijetlo (n.).

items.en: The village is quiet and green.
items.note: Neuter agreement: -o endings.

activities.title: Agreement in description
activities.intro: Match adjectives to gender, number and case while describing people and places.

questions.prompt: Ana je ___.
questions.explanation: Ana is feminine singular → visoka.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of kratak: Marko ima ___ kosu.
questions.explanation: kosa is feminine and here in the accusative after imati: kratka kosa → kratku kosu.

questions.prompt: What is the opposite of visok?
questions.explanation: visok = tall, nizak = short (of height).

questions.prompt: Ona ima ___ oči.
questions.explanation: oči is feminine plural → zelene oči (green eyes).

questions.prompt: Complete with the feminine form of duhovit: Moja sestra je jako ___.
questions.explanation: Feminine subject → duhovita: Moja sestra je jako duhovita.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'My city is big and beautiful.'
questions.answer: Moj grad je velik i lijep.
questions.explanation: grad is masculine → velik i lijep, no ending changes in the nominative.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'He wears glasses'?
questions.explanation: naočale is a plural-only noun; the accusative after nositi is naočale: Nosi naočale.

questions.prompt: Complete: Stan je mali, ali ___. (bright, masculine)
questions.explanation: Masculine nominative: svijetao (note the -ije- spelling): Stan je mali, ali svijetao.

activities.title: Kakva je nova kolegica?
activities.intro: You are at a café with a friend who wants to hear all about your new colleague. Describe her looks and personality.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Čuo sam da imaš novu kolegicu. Kakva je?
lines.en: I heard you have a new colleague. What is she like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Zove se Ana. Jako je ljubazna i duhovita.
lines.en: Her name is Ana. She is very kind and funny.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A kako izgleda?
lines.en: And what does she look like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Visoka je i ima dugu smeđu kosu i plave oči.
lines.en: She is tall and has long brown hair and blue eyes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nosi li naočale?
lines.en: Does she wear glasses?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ne, ne nosi. Uvijek nosi elegantnu odjeću.
lines.en: No, she doesn't. She always wears elegant clothes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odakle je?
lines.en: Where is she from?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Iz Splita. Kaže da je Split ljepši od Zagreba!
lines.en: From Split. She says Split is more beautiful than Zagreb!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ha! A kakav je njezin novi stan?
lines.en: Ha! And what is her new flat like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mali je, ali svijetao i miran. Blizu je parka.
lines.en: It's small, but bright and quiet. It's near the park.

days.title: Listening + dictation
days.objective: Sharpen comprehension and spelling through dictation.
days.paretoFocus: Croatian's phonetic spelling makes dictation especially effective — what you hear is exactly what you write.

items: Re-listen to the words you misheard.

activities.title: Dictation day — write what you hear
activities.intro: Croatian spelling is phonetic: what you hear is exactly what you write. Dictation trains your ear and locks in the few genuinely tricky spots — č/ć, dž/đ and ije/je.

items.note: The golden rule of Croatian spelling — every sound has exactly one letter.
The dictation loop: play the sentence, say it aloud, then write it from memory.

items.hr: č vs ć: čaj, čovjek / kuća, noć
items.note: Both sound like 'ch' to beginners — learn which common words take which letter.
dž is written with two letters but is ONE sound.

items.hr: ije vs je: mlijeko, vrijeme, lijepo / djeca, čovjek, mjesto
items.en: milk, weather/time, beautiful / children, person, place
items.note: Long syllable = ije, short syllable = je. Listen for vowel length.

items.hr: Radit ću — two words, one sound
items.en: future forms are pronounced together but written separately
items.note: In dictation, remember the space: Radit ću, Gledat ćemo.
noć ends in ć — a classic dictation trap.

activities.title: Spelling dictation drill
activities.intro: Write the words exactly — diacritics count. This is your dictation without the audio: spell from meaning.

questions.prompt: Spell the Croatian word for 'milk'. (ije or je?)
questions.explanation: Long syllable → ije: mlijeko.

questions.prompt: Spell the Croatian word for 'children'. (ije or je?)
questions.explanation: Short syllable → je: djeca.

questions.prompt: Which spelling is correct for 'weather/time'?
questions.explanation: Long syllable → ije: vrijeme. (vreme is Serbian ekavian, not Croatian.)

questions.prompt: Which spelling is correct for 'house'?
questions.explanation: kuća takes ć. kuca (without the diacritic) means '(the dog) barks'!

questions.prompt: Spell the Croatian word for 'tea'. (č or ć?)
questions.explanation: čaj takes č — one of the most common č-words.

questions.prompt: You hear one word, 'raditću' — how do you WRITE it?
questions.explanation: The future is pronounced as one word but written as two: Radit ću.

questions.prompt: Spell 'Good night!' (two words, careful with the last letter)
questions.explanation: noć ends in ć: Laku noć!

questions.prompt: Spell the Croatian word for 'person/man'. (ije or je?)
questions.explanation: Short syllable → je, and it starts with č: čovjek.

questions.prompt: Rebuild the dictated sentence: 'The weather is nice today.'
questions.explanation: Both lijepo and vrijeme take ije: Danas je lijepo vrijeme.

days.title: Week review + 5-minute monologue
days.objective: Speak for 5 minutes mixing tenses.
days.paretoFocus: Longer monologues build the stamina and automaticity flashcards can't.
drills: Clear your due words in the Words tab.

activities.title: Your 5-minute monologue toolkit
activities.intro: Today you speak for five minutes mixing past, present and future. These frame phrases are the skeleton — hang this week's grammar on them.

items.en: First..., then..., after that..., in the end...
items.note: Sequence markers keep a long monologue organized.
Perfekt for everything in the past.
Present tense for now and for habits.

items.en: Tomorrow I will go to the seaside.
items.note: Futur I for plans — clitic in second position.

items.hr: prošli tjedan / ovaj tjedan / sljedeći tjedan
items.en: last week / this week / next week
items.note: Time anchors that instantly signal which tense is coming.

items.en: I think that... / I like... because...
items.note: Opinions and reasons stretch any topic by another minute.

items.hr: Kako se kaže...? / Kako da kažem...
items.en: How do you say...? / How shall I put it...
items.note: Repair phrases — keep talking instead of freezing.

activities.title: Week 5 mixed review
activities.intro: Futur, aspect, genitive, dative and locative — everything from this week in one drill.

questions.prompt: Sutra ___ učiti cijeli dan.
questions.explanation: Future plan → futur I: Sutra ću učiti cijeli dan.

questions.prompt: Complete with the genitive of voda: Popit ću čašu ___.
questions.explanation: čaša vode — 'of water' is genitive: voda → vode.

questions.prompt: Svako jutro ___ kavu.
questions.explanation: A habit ('every morning') → imperfective piti: Svako jutro pijem kavu.

questions.prompt: Complete with the dative of majka: Telefoniram ___.
questions.explanation: telefonirati + dative; majka → majci with sibilarization k → c.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is about the past?
questions.explanation: sam + participle radila = perfekt (past). ću + infinitive = future, radim = present.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'Yesterday I studied, and tomorrow I will work.'
questions.answer: Jučer sam učio, a sutra ću raditi.
questions.explanation: Both clitics (sam, ću) sit in second position inside their own clause: Jučer sam učio, a sutra ću raditi.

questions.prompt: Živim ___ Splitu.
questions.explanation: Cities take u + locative: u Splitu, u Zagrebu.

questions.prompt: Say 'I will not work on Saturday': ___ raditi u subotu.
questions.explanation: Negative future = stressed one-word form: Neću raditi u subotu.

questions.prompt: Napisala je pismo. — What does the aspect tell us?
questions.explanation: Perfective napisati = one whole completed event — the letter got written.

activities.title: Susjed na stubištu — small talk across three tenses
activities.intro: Your neighbour catches you on the staircase. A perfect low-stakes chance to mix past, present and future — exactly what your monologue needs.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan, susjede! Kako ste?
lines.en: Good day, neighbour! How are you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Dobro sam, hvala. Malo sam umoran jer sam cijeli tjedan puno učio.
lines.en: Good day! I'm well, thanks. A bit tired because I studied a lot all week.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A što ste učili?
lines.en: And what were you studying?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Učim hrvatski. Ovaj tjedan sam učio futur i padeže.
lines.en: I'm learning Croatian. This week I studied the future tense and cases.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlično govorite! Što ćete raditi za vikend?
lines.en: You speak excellently! What will you do at the weekend?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sutra ću se odmarati, a u nedjelju ću posjetiti prijatelje.
lines.en: Tomorrow I will rest, and on Sunday I will visit friends.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Lijepo! Mi ćemo ići na more ako bude lijepo vrijeme.
lines.en: Nice! We will go to the seaside if the weather is good.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sretan put! Vidjet ćemo se sljedeći tjedan.
lines.en: Have a good trip! We'll see each other next week.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hoćemo, naravno. Doviđenja!
lines.en: We will, of course. Goodbye!

days.title: Comparatives
days.objective: Compare things: -iji/-ši and 'nego/od'.
days.paretoFocus: Comparisons appear in opinions and shopping constantly; the core pattern is simple and high-yield.

activities.title: Comparatives — stariji, ljepši, bolji
activities.intro: Three regular patterns (-iji, -ji, -ši) plus four irregulars cover almost every comparison. The superlative is always naj- glued onto the comparative.

items.note: The default pattern: -iji; superlative = naj- + comparative.
Longer adjectives almost always take -iji.

items.hr: mlad → mlađi, jak → jači, skup → skuplji, tih → tiši
items.note: -ji fuses with the final consonant (iotation): d→đ, k→č, p→plj, h→š.

items.hr: lijep → ljepši, lak → lakši, mek → mekši
items.note: Exactly three adjectives take -ši — this is the whole list.

items.hr: dobar → bolji, loš → gori, velik → veći, mali → manji
items.note: The four irregulars — memorize them as words of their own.
od + genitive is the most common 'than'.
nego + nominative — same meaning, different construction.
Adverbs compare with the neuter form: mnogo → više, malo → manje, loše → gore.

items.hr: Ovo je najljepši grad na svijetu.
items.en: This is the most beautiful city in the world.
items.note: naj + ljepši, written as one word.

activities.title: Bigger, better, the best
activities.intro: Form comparatives and superlatives and compare with od/nego.

questions.prompt: What is the comparative of star?
questions.explanation: Default pattern -iji: star → stariji. najstariji is the superlative.

questions.prompt: Give the comparative of dobar (m. sg.):
questions.explanation: dobar is irregular: dobar → bolji → najbolji.

questions.prompt: Give the comparative of velik (m. sg.):
questions.explanation: velik is irregular: velik → veći → najveći.

questions.prompt: What is the comparative of mlad?
questions.explanation: -ji fuses with d → đ: mlad → mlađi.

questions.prompt: Kava je ___ od čaja.
questions.explanation: skup takes -ji with iotation p → plj: skuplji, feminine skuplja. Croatian never uses 'više' + adjective for comparison.

questions.prompt: What is the superlative of dobar (m. sg.)?
questions.explanation: Superlative = naj- + comparative: naj + bolji = najbolji, one word.

questions.prompt: Ivan je stariji ___ Marka.
questions.explanation: Marka is genitive, so the preposition must be od: stariji od Marka. (nego would take the nominative: nego Marko.)

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'My flat is smaller than yours.'
questions.answer: Moj stan je manji od tvoga.
questions.explanation: mali → manji (irregular); od + genitive tvoga.

questions.prompt: Danas se osjećam ___ nego jučer.
questions.explanation: 'I feel better' needs the ADVERB comparative (neuter form): dobro → bolje. bolji is the adjective.

days.title: Weather & seasons
days.objective: Discuss weather, seasons and clothing.
days.paretoFocus: Weather is a universal small-talk opener and recycles familiar vocabulary and verbs.

items: Re-test the weather words from memory.

activities.title: Weather & seasons — Kakvo je vrijeme?
activities.intro: Weather is the universal small-talk opener. Most phrases are impersonal ('It is sunny' = Sunčano je) or use padati/puhati ('rain falls', 'wind blows').

items.note: vrijeme means both 'weather' and 'time' — context decides.
Impersonal: adjective in neuter + je.
Literally 'rain/snow is falling' — kiša and snijeg are the subjects.
On the coast you'll hear the famous winds by name: bura and jugo.

items.hr: Vruće je. / Toplo je. / Hladno je.
items.en: It's hot. / It's warm. / It's cold.
items.note: The three temperature basics.
From magla (fog).
In a season: u proljeće, ljeti, u jesen, zimi.

items.hr: Ljeti je vruće, a zimi pada snijeg.
items.en: In summer it's hot, and in winter it snows.
items.note: ljeti and zimi are handy one-word adverbs.

items.hr: Kad je hladno, nosim jaknu i kapu.
items.en: When it's cold, I wear a jacket and a cap.
items.note: Weather links straight to clothing vocabulary.

activities.title: Weather talk drill
activities.intro: Seasons, weather phrases — and yesterday's comparatives recycled.

questions.prompt: How do you ask what the weather is like?
questions.explanation: kakvo asks 'what kind of' — Kakvo je vrijeme? (Koliko je sati? asks the TIME.)

questions.prompt: It's raining: Pada ___.
questions.explanation: Pada kiša — literally 'rain is falling'.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'It's sunny'?
questions.explanation: Impersonal weather: neuter adjective + je → Sunčano je.

questions.prompt: What is the Croatian word for 'winter'?
questions.explanation: zima = winter. Zimi = in winter.

questions.prompt: Which season comes after proljeće?
questions.explanation: proljeće (spring) → ljeto (summer) → jesen (autumn) → zima (winter).

questions.prompt: It's snowing: Pada ___. (careful: ije or je?)
questions.explanation: Long syllable → ije: snijeg. Pada snijeg = it's snowing.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'In summer it's hot, and in winter it's cold.'
questions.answer: Ljeti je vruće, a zimi je hladno.
questions.explanation: ljeti/zimi are one-word season adverbs; a contrasts the two halves.

questions.prompt: Puše jak ___.
questions.explanation: puhati goes with wind: Puše jak vjetar = a strong wind is blowing.

questions.prompt: Danas je ___ nego jučer. (warmer)
questions.explanation: Impersonal weather comparison uses the neuter/adverb comparative: toplo → toplije. Danas je toplije nego jučer.

days.title: Health & the body
days.objective: Explain how you feel and basic symptoms.
days.paretoFocus: 'Boli me…' is essential survival language for travel and reuses body-part vocabulary.

items: Re-test the body parts in the Words tab.

activities.title: Body parts & Boli me...
activities.intro: In Croatian your head hurts YOU: the body part is the subject and the person in pain is in the accusative — Boli me glava. Learn the ten core body parts and this pattern and you can explain any ache.

items.note: oči and uši are irregular plurals — among the oldest words in the language.
leđa is always plural: Bole me leđa.
Ten body parts cover nearly every complaint.
The body part is the SUBJECT; the person is accusative: boli + me/te/ga/je/nas/vas/ih.

items.hr: Bole me leđa. / Bole me zubi.
items.en: My back hurts. / My teeth hurt.
items.note: Plural body part → plural verb bole.

items.hr: Boli ga zub. / Boli je grlo.
items.en: His tooth hurts. / Her throat hurts.
items.note: ga = him, je = her — accusative clitics.

items.en: I have a fever. / I have a cold.
items.note: A woman says: Prehlađena sam.
From kašljati and kihati.

items.en: I need something for the pain.
items.note: protiv + genitive: protiv bolova, protiv kašlja.

activities.title: Symptoms & the boli pattern
activities.intro: Say what hurts — and make boli/bole agree.

questions.prompt: My head hurts: ___ me glava.
questions.explanation: Singular body part → boli: Boli me glava.

questions.prompt: ___ me leđa.
questions.explanation: leđa is plural, so the verb is plural too: Bole me leđa.

questions.prompt: What is the Croatian word for 'stomach'?
questions.explanation: trbuh = stomach/belly. Boli me trbuh = my stomach hurts.

questions.prompt: His tooth hurts: Boli ___ zub.
questions.explanation: The person in pain is an accusative clitic: ga = him. Boli ga zub.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I have a fever'?
questions.explanation: imati + accusative: temperatura → temperaturu. Imam temperaturu.

questions.prompt: What is the Croatian word for 'throat'?
questions.explanation: grlo = throat. Boli me grlo = I have a sore throat.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'My stomach has been hurting since yesterday.'
questions.explanation: boli + accusative me + subject trbuh; od jučer = since yesterday.

questions.prompt: Trebam nešto protiv ___.
questions.explanation: protiv takes the genitive: bolovi (pains) → protiv bolova.

questions.prompt: Someone says 'Prehlađena sam.' — who is speaking?
questions.explanation: The participle-like adjective agrees with the speaker: prehlađen (m.) / prehlađena (f.).

activities.title: U ljekarni — Boli me glava
activities.intro: You wake up with a headache and a sore throat, so you head to the pharmacy. Explain your symptoms and get something for the pain.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dobar dan. Trebam pomoć.
lines.en: Good day. I need help.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Izvolite, kako vam mogu pomoći?
lines.en: Good day! Go ahead, how can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Boli me glava i grlo. Mislim da imam temperaturu.
lines.en: My head and throat hurt. I think I have a fever.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Od kada imate simptome?
lines.en: Since when have you had the symptoms?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Od jučer. Loše sam spavao i jako kašljem.
lines.en: Since yesterday. I slept badly and I'm coughing a lot.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Jeste li alergični na neke lijekove?
lines.en: Are you allergic to any medicines?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ne, nisam. Trebam nešto protiv bolova.
lines.en: No, I'm not. I need something for the pain.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Uzmite ove tablete protiv bolova i sirup za kašalj. Jedna tableta svakih osam sati.
lines.en: Take these painkillers and cough syrup. One tablet every eight hours.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala. Koliko košta?
lines.en: Thank you. How much does it cost?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Deset eura. Ako ne bude bolje za tri dana, idite liječniku.
lines.en: Ten euros. If it isn't better in three days, go to the doctor.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hoću. Hvala vam puno. Doviđenja!
lines.en: I will. Thank you very much. Goodbye!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Doviđenja i brzo ozdravite!
lines.en: Goodbye, and get well soon!

days.title: Travel & transport
days.objective: Buy tickets and navigate transport.
days.paretoFocus: Transport vocabulary + directions cover the travel experience that motivates many learners.

activities.title: Buying a ticket — key phrases
activities.intro: Today you learn to buy a ticket and navigate a Croatian bus or train station. The pattern 'karta za + accusative' (a ticket to...) plus a handful of station words (polazak, dolazak, peron, kolodvor) covers almost every ticket-window conversation.

items.note: karta za + city in the accusative

items.en: When does the bus to Split leave?
From which platform does it leave?
How much does a ticket cost?
How long does the ride take?

activities.title: At the ticket window
activities.intro: Practise the ticket-buying phrases: asking about departures, platforms and prices.

questions.prompt: How do you ask when the bus to Split leaves?
questions.explanation: 'Kada polazi...?' asks about departure time; 'gdje' asks where, 'koliko košta' asks about price.

questions.prompt: Jednu ___ za Zagreb, molim. (a ticket)
questions.explanation: 'karta' is the object of the request, so it takes the accusative: kartu.

questions.prompt: What does 'povratna karta' mean?
questions.explanation: povratna karta = return ticket; jednosmjerna karta = one-way ticket.

questions.prompt: S kojeg ___ polazi vlak? (platform)
questions.explanation: After 's kojeg' (from which) the noun takes the genitive: peron → perona.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'When does the next bus to Split leave?'
questions.answer: Kada polazi sljedeći autobus za Split?
questions.explanation: Question word first, then the verb: Kada polazi sljedeći autobus za Split?

questions.prompt: Where do you buy a train ticket?
questions.explanation: željeznički kolodvor = train station; ljekarna = pharmacy, pekara = bakery.

questions.prompt: Autobus polazi u osam, a ___ u Split je u podne. (arrival)
questions.explanation: polazak = departure, dolazak = arrival.

questions.prompt: The clerk says: 'Peron broj dva.' What did you learn?
options: The bus leaves from platform two.
The bus leaves at two o'clock.
questions.answer: The bus leaves from platform two.
questions.explanation: peron = platform, broj dva = number two.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'How much does a return ticket to Rijeka cost?'
questions.answer: Koliko košta povratna karta za Rijeku?
questions.explanation: Koliko košta + subject: Koliko košta povratna karta za Rijeku?

activities.title: Buying a bus ticket to Split
activities.intro: You are at the bus station in Zagreb and want a return ticket to Split. Read your lines aloud, then cover the Croatian and reconstruct them from the English.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dobar dan. Jednu kartu za Split, molim.
lines.en: Good day. One ticket to Split, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan. Jednosmjernu ili povratnu?
lines.en: Good day. One-way or return?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Povratnu, molim. Koliko košta?
lines.en: Return, please. How much does it cost?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Povratna karta košta trideset eura.
lines.en: A return ticket costs thirty euros.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: U redu. Kada polazi sljedeći autobus?
lines.en: All right. When does the next bus leave?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Polazi u devet i trideset, s perona broj četiri.
lines.en: It leaves at nine thirty, from platform number four.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A kada stiže u Split?
lines.en: And when does it arrive in Split?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Stiže u četrnaest sati. Vožnja traje oko četiri i pol sata.
lines.en: It arrives at 2 p.m. The ride takes about four and a half hours.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Izvrsno. Izvolite karticu.
lines.en: Excellent. Here is my card.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hvala. Izvolite kartu i račun. Sretan put!
lines.en: Thank you. Here is your ticket and receipt. Have a good trip!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala lijepa. Doviđenja!
lines.en: Thank you very much. Goodbye!

days.title: Instrumental case — intro
days.objective: Use the instrumental for 'with' and means.
days.paretoFocus: Instrumental covers 'with someone' and 'by (transport)' — common and reuses your travel theme ('autobusom').

activities.title: The instrumental — how and with whom
activities.intro: The instrumental case answers 'with whom?' and 'by what means?'. Endings: masculine and neuter take -om (vlak → vlakom), or -em after a soft consonant (č, ć, đ, ž, š, j, lj, nj: tramvaj → tramvajem, prijatelj → prijateljem); feminine -a becomes -om (sestra → sestrom). Use s/sa ONLY for company ('with a friend' = s prijateljem); for means of transport there is NO preposition: putujem vlakom, never 's vlakom'. Before s, š, z, ž (and in 'sa mnom'), s becomes sa.

items.note: no preposition for means of transport!
-em after the soft consonant j
an adverb — no case ending needed
-em after lj; s = with (company)
s → sa before s, š, z, ž

items.en: I travel to Split by train.
I am going to the cinema with a friend.

activities.title: Instrumental transformations
activities.intro: Turn nouns into the instrumental and choose when to use s/sa — and when to leave it out.

questions.prompt: Putujem ___ u Rijeku. (vlak)
questions.explanation: Means of transport: instrumental with no preposition — vlak → vlakom.

questions.prompt: Idem na posao ___. (autobus)
questions.explanation: autobus + -om = autobusom (by bus).

questions.prompt: How do you say 'by tram'?
questions.explanation: After the soft consonant j the ending is -em, and means of transport takes no preposition.

questions.prompt: Idem u kino s ___. (prijatelj)
questions.explanation: lj is a soft consonant, so prijatelj takes -em: s prijateljem.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'with my sister'?
questions.explanation: s becomes sa before s, š, z, ž; sestra → sestrom in the instrumental.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'I travel by car'?
questions.explanation: Means of transport = bare instrumental. 's autom' would mean the car is your travel companion!

questions.prompt: Pijem kavu s ___. (mlijeko)
questions.explanation: Neuter mlijeko takes -om: kava s mlijekom = coffee with milk.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Tomorrow I am travelling to Zagreb by bus.'
questions.explanation: Time word first, then verb, means (autobusom) and destination (u Zagreb).

questions.prompt: Ideš li ___ u grad? (with me)
questions.explanation: 'With me' is always 'sa mnom' — s becomes sa before the pronoun form mnom.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I am going to the seaside with a (female) friend.'
questions.explanation: na more = to the seaside; s prijateljicom = with a friend (instrumental).

days.title: Reading day
days.objective: Read a short text and mine it for language.
days.paretoFocus: Reading builds vocabulary in context and reinforces grammar passively.
drills: Read a short A1/A2 text twice.

items: Note the 5 best phrases and re-test tomorrow.

activities.title: Reading: Moje putovanje u Split
activities.intro: Read this short A1 text twice — first for the gist, then slowly. — MOJE PUTOVANJE U SPLIT: Zovem se Petra i živim u Zagrebu. Prošlog vikenda putovala sam u Split. Išla sam autobusom jer je karta jeftina. Autobus je polazio u sedam sati ujutro s glavnog kolodvora. Putovanje je trajalo pet sati. U Splitu sam šetala uz more i pila kavu s prijateljicom. Vrijeme je bilo sunčano i toplo. Navečer smo jele ribu u malom restoranu. Vratila sam se kući u nedjelju navečer. Bilo je predivno! — Now mine it: the phrases below are the ones worth stealing for your own stories.

items.note: genitive of time — a set phrase
female speaker: putovala; male: putovao

items.hr: polazio je u sedam sati ujutro
items.en: it departed at seven in the morning
I drank coffee with a friend
items.note: s + instrumental

items.hr: vrijeme je bilo sunčano i toplo
items.en: the weather was sunny and warm

items.note: kući = home(wards), no preposition

activities.title: Did you understand the text?
activities.intro: Comprehension questions about Petra's trip, plus the best phrases from the text.

questions.prompt: Kamo je Petra putovala prošlog vikenda?
questions.explanation: The text says: 'Prošlog vikenda putovala sam u Split.'

questions.prompt: Kako je Petra putovala?
questions.explanation: 'Išla sam autobusom' — she went by bus (instrumental of means).

questions.prompt: Zašto je išla autobusom?
questions.explanation: '...jer je karta jeftina' — because the ticket is cheap.

questions.prompt: Putovanje je ___ pet sati. (lasted)
questions.explanation: trajati = to last; neuter past form because 'putovanje' is neuter: trajalo.

questions.prompt: Što je Petra radila u Splitu?
options: Šetala je uz more i pila kavu.
questions.answer: Šetala je uz more i pila kavu.
questions.explanation: 'U Splitu sam šetala uz more i pila kavu s prijateljicom.'

questions.prompt: Vratila sam se ___ u nedjelju navečer. (home)
questions.explanation: 'kući' = home(wards) — a set adverbial form, no preposition needed.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'The weather was sunny and warm.'
questions.answer: Vrijeme je bilo sunčano i toplo.
questions.explanation: Subject + je + bilo (neuter, agreeing with vrijeme) + adjectives.

questions.prompt: What does 'predivno' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Bilo je predivno!' = It was wonderful!

questions.prompt: Pila sam kavu s ___. (prijateljica)
questions.explanation: s + instrumental: prijateljica → prijateljicom.

days.title: Week review + conversation
days.objective: Use travel, health and weather in a live conversation.
days.paretoFocus: Theme-mixing conversation simulates real life, where topics don't come pre-sorted.
drills: Clear your due words in the Words tab.

activities.title: Week-review conversation starters
activities.intro: Real conversations mix topics — travel, weather, health — without warning. These frames let you carry a ten-minute chat about your week using everything from days 36-43.

items.note: to a woman: Jesi li putovala negdje?

items.en: What are you planning for next week?

activities.title: Week 6 mixed review
activities.intro: Travel, instrumental, health and weather — all in one exercise, the way real life serves them.

questions.prompt: U subotu sam išao ___ u Rijeku. (vlak)
questions.explanation: Means of transport: instrumental without a preposition — vlakom.

questions.prompt: How do you ask a friend what the weather was like?
questions.explanation: kakvo = what kind of; 'Kakvo je bilo vrijeme?' = What was the weather like?

questions.prompt: Ujutro je ___ kiša, ali poslije je bilo sunčano. (padati, past)
questions.explanation: kiša is feminine, so the past participle is padala: Padala je kiša.

questions.prompt: What does 'Boljelo me grlo' mean?
questions.explanation: boljeti = to hurt; grlo = throat. Literally: 'The throat hurt me.'

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Now I feel much better.'
questions.explanation: The clitic 'se' goes in second position: Sada se osjećam...

questions.prompt: Kupio sam povratnu ___ za Split. (ticket)
questions.explanation: Direct object in the accusative: karta → kartu.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
options: Putovao sam s autobusom s prijateljem.
questions.explanation: Means = bare instrumental (autobusom); company = s + instrumental (s prijateljem).

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'What are you planning for next week?'
questions.explanation: Question word + verb + za + accusative time phrase.

activities.title: Catching up on the week
activities.intro: A friendly conversation that hops between the week's themes: travel, weather, health and plans. Play your side aloud, then swap roles.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bok! Kako si? Kakav ti je bio tjedan?
lines.en: Hi! How are you? How was your week?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bok! Dobro sam, hvala. Tjedan je bio zanimljiv — puno sam učio.
lines.en: Hi! I'm fine, thanks. The week was interesting — I studied a lot.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Jesi li putovao negdje?
lines.en: Did you travel anywhere?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da, u subotu sam išao vlakom u Rijeku s prijateljem.
lines.en: Yes, on Saturday I went to Rijeka by train with a friend.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super! Kakvo je bilo vrijeme?
lines.en: Great! What was the weather like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ujutro je padala kiša, ali poslije je bilo sunčano.
lines.en: In the morning it was raining, but later it was sunny.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A kako se osjećaš? Prošli tjedan si bio bolestan.
lines.en: And how are you feeling? Last week you were ill.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Sada se osjećam puno bolje. Boljelo me grlo, ali više me ne boli.
lines.en: Now I feel much better. I had a sore throat, but it doesn't hurt any more.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Drago mi je! Što planiraš za sljedeći tjedan?
lines.en: I'm glad! What are you planning for next week?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Želim više razgovarati na hrvatskom i naučiti nove riječi.
lines.en: I want to speak more Croatian and learn new words.

days.title: Expressing opinions
days.objective: Give and justify opinions: mislim da, po meni.
days.paretoFocus: Opinion frames let you say something about anything — the engine of interesting conversation.

items: Re-test the opinion phrases from memory.

activities.title: Expressing opinions
activities.intro: Opinion frames let you say something about anything. Model sentence: 'Mislim da je Zagreb lijep grad jer ima puno parkova.' (I think Zagreb is a beautiful city because it has many parks.) Learn the frame, then swap in your own topic and reason.

items.note: the workhorse — always followed by a full clause
s + instrumental again!
give a reason after every opinion

activities.title: Opinions, agreement and polite disagreement
activities.intro: Practise stating opinions, agreeing and disagreeing without starting a fight.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I think that...'?
questions.explanation: misliti = to think; 'Mislim da...' introduces an opinion.

questions.prompt: ___ se s tobom. (I agree)
questions.explanation: slagati se = to agree; Slažem se s tobom = I agree with you.

questions.prompt: What does 'po meni' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Po meni' literally 'by me' = in my opinion (informal).

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I think that coffee in Croatia is very good.'
questions.answer: Mislim da je kava u Hrvatskoj jako dobra.
questions.explanation: After 'da' the clitic 'je' comes immediately: Mislim da je kava...

questions.prompt: Ne ___ se — po meni je vlak bolji od autobusa. (agree)
questions.explanation: Ne slažem se = I don't agree.

questions.prompt: Which is a POLITE way to disagree?
questions.explanation: 'Maybe, but I think differently' softens the disagreement; the other options are rude.

questions.prompt: Zašto tako ___? (misliti, ti)
questions.explanation: misliti in the ti-form: misliš. 'Zašto tako misliš?' = Why do you think so?

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'In my opinion, Split is the most beautiful city.'
questions.answer: Po mom mišljenju, Split je najljepši grad.
questions.explanation: Po mom mišljenju + subject + je + superlative adjective + noun.

questions.prompt: Your friend says 'Imaš pravo.' What did they just do?
options: They agreed that you are right.
They asked you to turn right.
They said you have the law.
questions.answer: They agreed that you are right.
questions.explanation: Imaš pravo / U pravu si = You're right.

days.title: The vocative & addressing people
days.objective: Use the vocative to call or address someone.
days.paretoFocus: The vocative is simple but constant in conversation (Marko → Marko!, gospodine!) and makes you sound natural.

items: Address a friend and a stranger correctly.
Note the vocative is only for addressing.

activities.title: The vocative — calling and addressing
activities.intro: The vocative is the case for calling or addressing someone directly: 'Ivane, dođi!' Rules of thumb: masculine nouns in a hard consonant add -e (gospodin → gospodine!), soft-consonant nouns add -u (prijatelj → prijatelju!), feminine common nouns in -a switch to -o (gospođa → gospođo!), while most personal names like Marko, Ana or Ivana stay unchanged (Ivan → Ivane! is the classic exception). It is used ONLY for addressing — never as a subject or object.

items.note: names ending in -o don't change
hard consonant + -e
most female names stay the same
-a → -o for common nouns
-ica → -ice
-u after the soft consonant lj
masculine nouns in -a stay unchanged
k, g, h soften to č, ž, š — hence 'Bože moj!' (my God!)

activities.title: Address people correctly
activities.intro: Call friends, strangers and professionals with the right vocative form.

questions.prompt: You want to call your friend Ivan across the street. You shout:
questions.explanation: Masculine names in a hard consonant take -e in the vocative: Ivan → Ivane!

questions.prompt: Dobar dan, ___! (gospodin)
questions.explanation: gospodin → gospodine! (sir) — the standard polite address to a man.

questions.prompt: Oprostite, ___! (gospođa)
questions.explanation: Feminine common nouns in -a take -o: gospođa → gospođo!

questions.prompt: What is the vocative of 'Marko'?
questions.explanation: Names ending in -o stay unchanged in the vocative: Marko → Marko!

questions.prompt: ___, dođi ovamo! (prijatelj)
questions.explanation: After a soft consonant (lj) the vocative ending is -u: prijatelju!

questions.prompt: When do you use the vocative?
options: Only when addressing or calling someone directly.
For the subject of a sentence.
questions.answer: Only when addressing or calling someone directly.
questions.explanation: The vocative has one job: direct address. It never marks subjects or objects.

questions.prompt: Hvala vam, ___! (doktor)
questions.explanation: doktor → doktore! Hard consonant + -e.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Mr Horvat, your ticket is here.'
questions.answer: Gospodine Horvat, vaša karta je ovdje.
questions.explanation: The title takes the vocative (gospodine), the surname stays unchanged.

questions.prompt: What does 'Bože moj!' mean?
questions.explanation: Bog → Bože! — g softens to ž in the vocative. A very common exclamation.

days.title: Storytelling with aspect
days.objective: Tell a short story using past tense and aspect.
days.paretoFocus: Storytelling integrates tense and aspect — the clearest sign you've internalised the verb system.
drills: Choose imperfective for background, perfective for events.

items: Note one aspect choice to double-check.

activities.title: Storytelling with aspect
activities.intro: A Croatian story runs on two engines: the imperfective paints the background (what was going on), the perfective drives the plot (what happened next). Model story: 'Dok sam jučer čekao tramvaj, čitao sam novine. Odjednom sam vidio staru prijateljicu. Popili smo kavu i dugo razgovarali. Na kraju smo se dogovorili za večeru.' Background = čekao, čitao; events = vidio, popili, dogovorili se.

items.note: imperfective / perfective

items.en: to watch / to take a look
While I was waiting for the bus...
items.note: imperfective = background
perfective = event
connectors that hold a story together

activities.title: Background or event?
activities.intro: Choose the right aspect: imperfective for the scenery, perfective for the plot.

questions.prompt: 'Dok sam ___ autobus, čitao sam novine.' (While I was waiting for the bus, I was reading the paper.)
questions.explanation: Ongoing background action after 'dok' → imperfective čekati: čekao.

questions.prompt: 'Jučer sam ___ cijelu knjigu.' (Yesterday I read the whole book — and finished it.)
questions.explanation: A completed result (the WHOLE book) → perfective pročitati: pročitao.

questions.prompt: Prvo sam ___ kavu, zatim sam otišao na posao. (popiti, past, m.)
questions.explanation: A finished event in a sequence → perfective popiti: popio sam kavu.

questions.prompt: Which verb is PERFECTIVE?
questions.explanation: kupiti = to buy (one completed act); kupovati and gledati are imperfective.

questions.prompt: Svaki dan ___ novine, ali jučer nisam imao vremena. (čitati, ja, present)
questions.explanation: Habitual action (every day) → imperfective present: čitam.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'While I was waiting for the tram, I saw a friend.'
questions.answer: Dok sam čekao tramvaj, vidio sam prijatelja.
questions.explanation: dok + imperfective background (čekao), then the perfective-style event (vidio sam).

questions.prompt: Which sentence means you FINISHED the letter?
