questions.explanation: Perfective napisati = the letter got written; pisao sam = I was writing (maybe never finished).

questions.prompt: Odjednom je ___ moj brat. (doći, past, m.)
questions.explanation: A sudden plot event → perfective doći: došao je.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'In the end we went home.'
questions.explanation: Na kraju (in the end) + smo (clitic in second position) + otišli kući.

days.title: Phone & messages
days.objective: Handle a simple call and write a short message.
days.paretoFocus: Set phrases for calls/messages are formulaic and high-utility.

items: Note the message phrases for tomorrow.

activities.title: Phone openers and closers
activities.intro: Phone Croatian is wonderfully formulaic: a fixed opener, a fixed closer, and a few set moves in between. Learn these as whole chunks — on the phone there is no body language to help you.

items.note: ovdje = 'here' — the standard way to identify yourself

items.en: Good day, this is Marko Horvat. (formal opener)

items.hr: Mogu li razgovarati s gospođom Kovač?
items.en: May I speak with Mrs Kovač?
items.note: razgovarati s + instrumental

items.en: He/She is not here at the moment.
Talk to you soon! (lit. 'we'll hear each other')

activities.title: On the phone
activities.intro: Openers, closers and the moves in between — plus writing a short message.

questions.prompt: Your phone rings. How do you answer?
questions.explanation: Halo? (or Molim?) is the standard way to answer the phone.

questions.prompt: Dobar dan, ___ Ana Kovač. (this is / '...speaking')
questions.explanation: On the phone you identify yourself with 'ovdje + name': Ovdje Ana Kovač.

questions.prompt: What does 'Mogu li razgovarati s gospodinom Horvatom?' mean?
options: May I speak with Mr Horvat?
Can Mr Horvat call me back?
questions.answer: May I speak with Mr Horvat?
questions.explanation: Mogu li + infinitive = May I...; razgovarati s + instrumental = speak with.

questions.prompt: Mogu li ostaviti ___? (a message)
questions.explanation: poruka in the accusative: Mogu li ostaviti poruku?

questions.prompt: Someone calls asking for a person you've never heard of. You say:
questions.explanation: krivi broj = wrong number.

questions.prompt: Nije tu trenutačno? U redu, nazvat ću ___. (later)
questions.explanation: Nazvat ću kasnije = I'll call back later.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'May I speak with Mrs Kovač?'
questions.answer: Mogu li razgovarati s gospođom Kovač?
questions.explanation: Mogu li + infinitive + s + instrumental (gospođom); the surname stays unchanged.

questions.prompt: How do you say goodbye to a friend on the phone?
questions.explanation: Čujemo se! = 'we'll hear each other' — the standard friendly phone sign-off.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I want to reserve a table for two people.'
questions.answer: Želim rezervirati stol za dvije osobe.
questions.explanation: želim + infinitive; za + accusative (dvije osobe).

activities.title: Booking a table by phone
activities.intro: You call a restaurant in Split to book a table for tonight. Practise until you can do your side without reading — on a real call you won't have subtitles.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Restoran Marjan, izvolite?
lines.en: Restaurant Marjan, how can I help you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dobar dan. Želim rezervirati stol za večeras, molim.
lines.en: Good day. I'd like to reserve a table for tonight, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Naravno. Za koliko osoba?
lines.en: Of course. For how many people?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Za dvije osobe, molim.
lines.en: For two people, please.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: U koliko sati?
lines.en: At what time?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: U osam sati, ako je moguće.
lines.en: At eight o'clock, if possible.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Može. Na koje ime?
lines.en: That works. Under what name?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Na ime Horvat. H-O-R-V-A-T.
lines.en: Under the name Horvat. H-O-R-V-A-T.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: U redu. Stol za dvoje večeras u dvadeset sati, na ime Horvat.
lines.en: All right. A table for two tonight at 8 p.m., under the name Horvat.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala lijepa. Doviđenja!
lines.en: Thank you very much. Goodbye!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hvala Vama. Doviđenja i vidimo se večeras!
lines.en: Thank you. Goodbye, and see you tonight!

days.title: Listening sprint
days.objective: Stretch comprehension with faster input.
days.paretoFocus: Gradually increasing audio speed trains real-world listening, the skill learners most often lack.
drills: Play an A1.hr unit audio at full speed, without reading.
Re-listen while reading the unit text.

activities.title: Listening sprint — radio bulletin
activities.intro: Today's routine: (1) listen (or read aloud fast) WITHOUT looking at the text, (2) re-listen while reading, (3) shadow one minute closely. Practice transcript — a short radio bulletin: 'Dobro jutro! Ovdje Radio Zagreb. Danas je četvrtak, deveti srpnja. Vrijeme je sunčano, temperatura je dvadeset osam stupnjeva. Autobusi i tramvaji voze normalno, ali vlak za Split kasni dvadeset minuta. Želimo vam ugodan dan!' The phrases below are your survival kit when real-speed Croatian outruns you.

items.note: ask about any word this way

items.en: the train is twenty minutes late
We wish you a pleasant day!

activities.title: What did the radio say?
activities.intro: Comprehension check on the bulletin, plus your listening survival phrases.

questions.prompt: Koji je dan u radijskim vijestima?
questions.explanation: 'Danas je četvrtak, deveti srpnja.' — Today is Thursday, July 9th.

questions.prompt: Kakvo je vrijeme danas prema vijestima?
questions.explanation: 'Vrijeme je sunčano' — the weather is sunny.

questions.prompt: Vlak za Split ___ dvadeset minuta. (is late)
questions.explanation: kasniti = to be late; vlak kasni dvadeset minuta.

questions.prompt: What does 'Možete li govoriti sporije?' mean?
questions.explanation: sporije = more slowly — your best friend during a listening sprint.

questions.prompt: Oprostite, možete li ___? (repeat)
questions.explanation: ponoviti = to repeat; Možete li ponoviti?

questions.prompt: Kolika je temperatura prema vijestima?
questions.explanation: 'temperatura je dvadeset osam stupnjeva' = 28 degrees.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I didn't understand — can you repeat that?'
questions.explanation: Nisam razumio (f.: razumjela) + možete li + infinitive.

questions.prompt: Autobusi i tramvaji voze ___. (normally)
questions.explanation: voze normalno = are running normally.

days.title: Week review + mock interview
days.objective: Answer common questions about yourself fluently.
days.paretoFocus: Rehearsing high-frequency questions builds the automatic responses real conversations demand.
drills: Answer 10 common questions about your life.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.

activities.title: Ten questions about you — answer templates
activities.intro: These are the questions every Croatian conversation partner asks in the first five minutes. Build a personal answer for each template now, say it aloud, and time yourself — the goal is an automatic answer within two seconds.

items.en: What is your name? → My name is...
Where are you from? → I am from...

items.hr: Gdje živite? → Živim u Zagrebu, blizu centra.
items.en: Where do you live? → I live in Zagreb, near the centre.

items.hr: Čime se bavite? → Radim kao... / Ja sam student.
items.en: What do you do? → I work as... / I am a student.
items.note: čime = instrumental of što!

items.hr: Koliko dugo učite hrvatski? → Učim hrvatski već sedam tjedana.
items.en: How long have you been learning Croatian? → I've been learning Croatian for seven weeks now.

items.hr: Zašto učite hrvatski? → Jer volim Hrvatsku i želim razgovarati s ljudima.
items.en: Why are you learning Croatian? → Because I love Croatia and want to talk with people.

items.hr: Što volite raditi u slobodno vrijeme? → Volim čitati, kuhati i šetati.
items.en: What do you like doing in your free time? → I like reading, cooking and walking.

items.hr: Što ste radili jučer? → Jučer sam radio, a navečer sam učio hrvatski.
items.en: What did you do yesterday? → Yesterday I worked, and in the evening I studied Croatian.
items.note: female speaker: radila, učila

items.hr: Kakvi su vaši planovi za vikend? → Za vikend planiram putovati u Split.
items.en: What are your plans for the weekend? → For the weekend I plan to travel to Split.

items.hr: Recite mi nešto o svojoj obitelji. → Imam brata i sestru.
items.en: Tell me something about your family. → I have a brother and a sister.

activities.title: Interview warm-up
activities.intro: Sharpen the building blocks of your answers before the mock interview below.

questions.prompt: 'Čime se bavite?' asks about your:
questions.explanation: Čime se bavite? (lit. 'with what do you occupy yourself?') = What do you do for a living?

questions.prompt: Kako se zovete? — "___ se Ana." (I am called)
questions.explanation: zvati se: Zovem se Ana = My name is Ana.

questions.prompt: Učim hrvatski ___ sedam tjedana. (already / for ... now)
questions.explanation: već + present tense = 'have been ...ing for': Učim već sedam tjedana.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I live in Zagreb, near the centre.'
questions.explanation: u + locative (Zagrebu); blizu + genitive (centra).

questions.prompt: Best answer to 'Zašto učite hrvatski?':
questions.explanation: zašto → jer: Why → Because I want to talk with people.

questions.prompt: Za vikend ___ putovati u Split. (planirati, ja)
questions.explanation: planirati + infinitive: planiram putovati = I plan to travel.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'What do you like doing in your free time?'
questions.answer: Što volite raditi u slobodno vrijeme?
questions.explanation: Što + volite + infinitive + u slobodno vrijeme (accusative).

questions.prompt: 'Odakle ste?' — which is a correct answer?
questions.explanation: odakle → iz + genitive: iz Amerike = from America.

questions.prompt: Želim razgovarati s ___. (ljudi — people, instrumental)
questions.explanation: ljudi → ljudima in the instrumental plural: razgovarati s ljudima.

activities.title: Mock interview — ten questions about your life
activities.intro: The final rehearsal of Phase 1: a partner interviews you with the highest-frequency personal questions. The Vi lines are templates — replace the details with YOUR facts and answer each question aloud within two seconds, without reading.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Kako se zovete?
lines.en: Good day! What is your name?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Zovem se Ana Smith.
lines.en: Good day! My name is Ana Smith.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odakle ste?
lines.en: Where are you from?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja sam iz Amerike, iz Chicaga.
lines.en: I am from America, from Chicago.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gdje sada živite?
lines.en: Where do you live now?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Živim u Zagrebu, u malom stanu blizu centra.
lines.en: I live in Zagreb, in a small flat near the centre.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Čime se bavite?
lines.en: What do you do for a living?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Radim kao programerka. Posao mi je zanimljiv.
lines.en: I work as a programmer. My job is interesting.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Koliko dugo učite hrvatski?
lines.en: How long have you been learning Croatian?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Učim hrvatski već sedam tjedana, svaki dan po sat vremena.
lines.en: I've been learning Croatian for seven weeks now, an hour every day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Zašto učite hrvatski?
lines.en: Why are you learning Croatian?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jer volim Hrvatsku i želim razgovarati s ljudima na hrvatskom.
lines.en: Because I love Croatia and want to talk with people in Croatian.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Što volite raditi u slobodno vrijeme?
lines.en: What do you like doing in your free time?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Volim čitati, kuhati i šetati uz more.
lines.en: I like reading, cooking and walking along the sea.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Što ste radili jučer?
lines.en: What did you do yesterday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jučer sam radio, a navečer sam gledao film i učio hrvatski.
lines.en: Yesterday I worked, and in the evening I watched a film and studied Croatian.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kakvi su vaši planovi za vikend?
lines.en: What are your plans for the weekend?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Za vikend planiram putovati vlakom u Split s prijateljima.
lines.en: For the weekend I plan to travel to Split by train with friends.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlično govorite hrvatski! Hvala vam na razgovoru.
lines.en: You speak Croatian excellently! Thank you for the conversation.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala lijepa! Bilo mi je drago. Doviđenja!
lines.en: Thank you very much! It was my pleasure. Goodbye!

days.title: Vocabulary consolidation
days.objective: Lock in your most useful ~600 words.
days.paretoFocus: Reviewing the high-frequency core makes the 20% of words that do 80% of the work automatic.
drills: Clear every due word in the Words tab.
Re-learn the words you keep missing.
Use 10 review words in fresh sentences.

items: Test yourself on 20 random core words.

activities.title: Core 600 in action
activities.intro: Today you lock in the words that do 80% of the work. Each sentence recycles several high-frequency words and one core grammar point. Read each aloud, cover the English, translate back — and star anything you hesitate on.

items.hr: Svaki dan ujutro pijem kavu i jedem kruh sa sirom.
items.en: Every morning I drink coffee and eat bread with cheese.
items.note: pijem/jedem + accusative (kava → kavu); sa + instrumental (sir → sirom).

items.hr: Radim u gradu, a moja žena radi kod kuće.
items.en: I work in the city, and my wife works at home.
items.note: u gradu = locative for location; kod kuće = fixed phrase 'at home'.

items.hr: Imam brata i sestru, ali nemam djece.
items.en: I have a brother and a sister, but I don't have children.
items.note: imati + accusative; after nemam the noun goes to the genitive (djece).

items.hr: Idem u trgovinu jer trebam mlijeko, jaja i vodu.
items.en: I'm going to the store because I need milk, eggs and water.
items.note: ići u + accusative shows direction; jer = because, the workhorse connector.

items.hr: Želim učiti hrvatski svaki dan po trideset minuta.
items.en: I want to study Croatian for thirty minutes every day.
items.note: željeti/htjeti + infinitive — one pattern, endless sentences.

items.hr: Mogu govoriti malo hrvatski, ali još ne razumijem sve.
items.en: I can speak a little Croatian, but I don't understand everything yet.
items.note: moći + infinitive; još ne = not yet. Your most honest and most useful sentence.

items.hr: Jučer sam bio umoran, pa sam spavao cijeli dan.
items.en: Yesterday I was tired, so I slept all day.
items.note: Perfekt: sam + participle. A woman says: bila sam umorna, pa sam spavala.

items.hr: Sutra ću kupiti kartu za autobus i putovati na more.
items.en: Tomorrow I'll buy a bus ticket and travel to the seaside.
items.note: Futur: ću + infinitive; karta za + accusative = a ticket to.

items.hr: Koliko košta ova knjiga? Nije skupa, samo deset eura.
items.en: How much does this book cost? It's not expensive, only ten euros.
items.note: Koliko košta? unlocks every shop; skup/skupa agrees with the noun's gender.

items.hr: Volim ljeto jer je toplo i sunčano, a zimu ne volim.
items.en: I love summer because it's warm and sunny, but winter I don't love.
items.note: voljeti + accusative: zima → zimu. Fronting 'zimu' works because the ending shows its role.

activities.title: Core vocabulary workout
activities.intro: Ten questions over the words and patterns you have been building since day 1. Aim for zero hesitation.

questions.prompt: Which word means 'bread'?
questions.explanation: kruh = bread, mlijeko = milk, sir = cheese, voda = water — all core shopping words.

questions.prompt: Pijem ___ svako jutro. (I drink coffee every morning)
questions.explanation: kava is the direct object, so it takes the accusative: feminine -a → -u.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Ne ___ sve. Govorite sporije, molim Vas.' (I don't understand everything.)
questions.explanation: razumjeti → razumijem. 'Ne razumijem' is your emergency brake in any conversation.

questions.prompt: What is the opposite of 'skup' (expensive)?
questions.explanation: skup ↔ jeftin (cheap). velik = big, star = old, topao = warm.

questions.prompt: Ask the price: '___ košta karta za Split?'
questions.explanation: Koliko košta...? = How much does ... cost? One of the seven question words.

questions.prompt: Put the sentence in order: 'Tomorrow I will buy a bus ticket.'
questions.explanation: Futur: ću sits in second position after 'Sutra', then the infinitive kupiti + object.

questions.prompt: Nemam ___. (I have no time)
questions.explanation: After the negative nemam, the noun takes the genitive: vrijeme → vremena.

questions.prompt: Say 'I want to study Croatian': '___ učiti hrvatski.'
questions.explanation: Želim (or hoću) + infinitive expresses a wish. Both are correct here.

questions.prompt: Which set contains ONLY food words?
questions.explanation: kruh/sir/jaje = bread/cheese/egg. The others are furniture-places, family, and adjectives.

questions.prompt: Order the sentence: 'Yesterday I drank coffee with a friend.' (male speaker)
questions.explanation: sam is a clitic: it goes in second position. kavu = accusative object, s prijateljem = instrumental.

days.title: Culture & register (ti/vi)
days.objective: Use the right level of formality in different settings.
days.paretoFocus: Register matters socially; knowing when to use ti vs vi and polite formulas is essential.
drills: Re-do a role-play formally then informally.

activities.title: Ti or Vi? The register rules
activities.intro: Croatian, like French or German, has two ways of saying 'you'. Using the wrong one is the most visible cultural mistake a learner can make — and the rules are learnable in one sitting.

items.hr: ti — prijatelji, obitelj, djeca, kolege vršnjaci
items.en: ti — friends, family, children, colleagues your own age
items.note: With ti the verb is 2nd person singular: Kako si? Što radiš?

items.hr: Vi — nepoznate osobe, starije osobe, službenici, šef
items.en: Vi — strangers, older people, officials, your boss
items.note: With Vi the verb is 2nd person PLURAL even for one person: Kako ste? Što radite?

items.hr: U kafiću i trgovini: konobaru i prodavačici kažete Vi.
items.en: In a café or a shop: use Vi with the waiter and the shop assistant.
items.note: 'Oprostite, koliko košta...?' is the safe opener everywhere in public.

items.hr: Kod liječnika, u banci, na šalteru: uvijek Vi.
items.en: At the doctor's, in the bank, at any counter: always Vi.
items.note: Institutions mean formal register — add gospodine (sir) / gospođo (madam).
The older or senior person normally offers the switch. Accept with: Može, naravno!
Too formal is merely polite; too informal can offend. Croatians will quickly invite you to 'ti'.

items.hr: Gospodine Horvat, izvolite! / Gospođo Kovač, izvolite!
items.en: Mr Horvat, here you are! / Mrs Kovač, here you are!
items.note: Polite address uses the vocative: gospodin → gospodine, gospođa → gospođo.

items.hr: U e-mailu: Poštovani gospodine... / Poštovana gospođo...
items.en: In an email: Dear Sir... / Dear Madam... (literally 'respected')
items.note: Poštovani (to a man) / Poštovana (to a woman) is THE formal written opener.

activities.title: Formal ↔ informal pairs
activities.intro: Every everyday phrase comes in two flavours. Learn them as pairs so you can switch register instantly — left is for friends (ti), right is for strangers and officials (Vi).

items.note: Bok also works for informal goodbye. Dobro jutro (morning) and dobra večer (evening) are also formal-friendly.
Same question, different verb form: si (sg.) vs ste (pl./formal).
The imperative gains -te in the formal/plural: oprosti → oprostite.
ti/Vam are dative forms: 'thanks TO you'. Capital V is polite in writing.
Literally 'I beg you' — te/Vas are accusative forms of ti/Vi.

items.en: Here you go / go ahead. (informal ↔ formal)
items.note: Heard constantly in shops and cafés — the waiter's 'Izvolite?' means 'What can I get you?'
Vidimo se is friendly and casual; doviđenja is neutral-formal and always safe.
The -te rule works for every command: sjedni/sjednite, uđi/uđite, pričekaj/pričekajte.

activities.title: Register check: ti or Vi?
activities.intro: Pick the right level of formality for each situation. In Croatia this is the difference between charming and rude.

questions.prompt: You walk into a bank. How do you greet the clerk?
questions.explanation: Institutions = formal register. Bok/Ej are for friends; Vidimo se is a goodbye.

questions.prompt: How do you ask a good friend how they are?
questions.explanation: A friend gets ti, so 2nd person singular: si. Kako ste? is for strangers or groups.

questions.prompt: The formal goodbye you can use anywhere: '___!'
questions.explanation: Doviđenja (literally 'until seeing') is the safe, neutral-formal goodbye.

questions.prompt: To your boss: 'Kako ___?' (How are you?)
questions.explanation: Your boss gets Vi, and Vi takes the plural verb form: ste.

questions.prompt: A waiter comes to your table and says 'Izvolite?'. What does it mean here?
questions.explanation: Izvolite is the formal 'go ahead / here you are' — from a waiter it invites your order.

questions.prompt: Order the polite offer to switch to informal address: 'Shall we switch to ti?'
questions.explanation: Možemo li...? = Can we...? The set phrase is 'prijeći na ti' — to cross over to ti.

questions.prompt: Thank an official politely: 'Hvala ___, gospodine!'
questions.explanation: Hvala + dative: ti (informal) → Vam (formal). Written with capital V out of politeness.

questions.prompt: With whom can you safely use 'ti' from the first moment?
options: an elderly neighbour you just met
questions.explanation: Children are always addressed with ti. Officials, elders and staff get Vi until they offer otherwise.

questions.prompt: You are writing a formal email to Mrs Kovač. How do you open it?
questions.explanation: Formal written opener: Poštovana (to a woman) + gospođo (vocative of gospođa) + surname.

days.title: Common mistakes clinic
days.objective: Target the errors you personally keep making (often cases & aspect).
days.paretoFocus: Fixing your top 5 recurring mistakes yields more improvement than new material.

items: Write your 5 corrections where you'll see them tomorrow.

activities.title: The top 10 classic mistakes — and their fixes
activities.intro: Every English-speaking learner of Croatian makes these ten mistakes. Fixing them now, before they fossilise, is worth more than a week of new material. Read the correct version aloud twice; the wrong version is shown so your ear learns to reject it.

items.hr: ✓ Ja sam iz Amerike. — ✗ od Amerike
items.note: Origin = iz + genitive (iz Hrvatske, iz Amerike). 'od' means from a person or since a time: od majke, od ponedjeljka.
The direct object is NEVER nominative. Feminine -a → -u in the accusative: kava → kavu, voda → vodu.

items.hr: ✓ Živim u Zagrebu. — ✗ Živim u Zagreb
items.note: Static location = locative (u Zagrebu). 'u Zagreb' (accusative) means direction: Idem u Zagreb = I'm going TO Zagreb.

items.hr: ✓ Jučer sam radio. — ✗ Jučer radio sam
items.note: sam/si/je/smo/ste/su are clitics — they must sit in SECOND position in the sentence, right after the first word or phrase.
The past participle agrees with the speaker: -o for men (radio), -la for women (radila), -li/-le in the plural.

items.hr: ✓ Imam trideset godina. — ✗ Ja sam trideset
items.note: Age uses imati, not biti: literally 'I HAVE thirty years'. godina is genitive plural after numbers 5+.

items.hr: ✓ Nemam ništa. / Ne znam ništa. — ✗ Imam ništa
items.en: I have nothing. / I know nothing.
items.note: Croatian requires DOUBLE negation: a ni-word (ništa, nitko, nikad) always travels with a negated verb.
ne + imam fuses into one word: nemam, nemaš, nema, nemamo, nemate, nemaju. (Same trick: nisam, neću.)

items.hr: ✓ Radim na poslu. / Studiram na fakultetu. — ✗ u poslu
items.en: I'm at work. / I study at university.
items.note: Some places take na instead of u: na poslu, na fakultetu, na moru, na trgu. Learn them as fixed pairs.

items.hr: ✓ Učim hrvatski i radim. — ✗ Ja učim hrvatski i ja radim
items.en: I study Croatian and I work.
items.note: The verb ending already shows the subject, so repeating ja sounds foreign. Use pronouns only for emphasis or contrast.

activities.title: Mistake clinic: fix it fast
activities.intro: Each question hides one of the top-10 mistakes. Catch it, fix it, and explain it to yourself in one sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: Origin takes iz + genitive. 'od' is for people and starting times, not countries.

questions.prompt: Fix the object: 'Pijem ___.' (kava)
questions.explanation: Direct object → accusative: kava → kavu. Feminine -a becomes -u.

questions.prompt: 'I live in Split' is:
questions.explanation: Living somewhere = static location = locative: u Splitu. 'u Split' would mean motion towards Split.

questions.prompt: Put the clitic where it belongs: 'Yesterday I watched a film.' (male speaker)
questions.explanation: sam is a clitic and must stand in second position: Jučer | sam | gledao film.

questions.prompt: A woman says 'I worked': '___ sam.'
questions.explanation: The participle agrees with the speaker's gender: radio (m) → radila (f).

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I am 30 years old'?
questions.explanation: Age uses imati + genitive plural: imam trideset godina — literally 'I have thirty of years'.

questions.prompt: Say 'I know nothing': 'Ne ___ ništa.'
questions.explanation: Double negation is obligatory: ništa demands a negated verb — ne znam ništa.

questions.prompt: The correct negative of 'imam' is:
questions.explanation: ne + imam fuses into nemam. Only three verbs fuse like this: nemam, nisam, neću.

questions.prompt: Which location phrase is WRONG?
questions.explanation: posao takes na: na poslu = at work. na fakultetu, na moru, na trgu also take na; škola takes u.

questions.prompt: Say it like a native — no unnecessary pronouns: 'I study Croatian and work in the city.'
questions.explanation: No 'ja' needed — the -m endings already say 'I'. Repeating ja before every verb sounds foreign.

days.title: Free conversation day
days.objective: Have an unscripted 15-minute conversation.
days.paretoFocus: Unscripted speaking is the real test; it builds the spontaneity that defines functional A1+.
drills: Push to use past, present and future.

items: Look up the words you lacked.

activities.title: Conversation lifelines
activities.intro: Free conversation is today's mission. These ten phrases keep you IN the conversation when you get stuck — asking, stalling, reacting and passing the ball back. Fluent use of lifelines beats a bigger vocabulary.

items.en: How do you say 'weekend' in Croatian?
items.note: Your #1 lifeline — it turns every gap into a mini-lesson. (Answer: vikend!)

items.hr: Možeš li ponoviti? / Možete li ponoviti, molim Vas?
items.note: Yesterday's register rules apply mid-conversation too.
sporije = comparative of sporo (slowly). Nobody minds being asked.
A stall phrase buys you 5 seconds without breaking the flow.
Adjective agrees with the speaker — like the past participle.
Što znači...? asks for meaning; kako se kaže...? asks for the word. Different directions!
The smallest sentence in Croatian — and the one that keeps every conversation alive.
Reactions make you a partner, not an interviewee. Ma nemoj! is friendly disbelief.

items.hr: Mislim da je to dobra ideja.
items.en: I think that's a good idea.
items.note: Mislim da... upgrades any statement into an opinion.
Colloquial and very Croatian — literally 'I don't have a concept'. Note the genitive: pojma.

activities.title: Free conversation: no script, just talk
activities.intro: This is a model of an unscripted chat — notice how 'Vi' (you) uses lifelines, mixes past, present and future, and keeps the ball moving. Read it aloud, then have YOUR version with a partner or tutor for 15 minutes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bok! Danas nema plana — samo razgovaramo. Kako si?
lines.en: Hi! No plan today — we're just talking. How are you?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Super, hvala! Malo sam umoran, ali sretan. A ti?
lines.en: Great, thanks! I'm a bit tired but happy. And you?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: I ja sam dobro. Što si radio jučer?
lines.en: I'm well too. What did you do yesterday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Jučer sam radio, a navečer sam gledao film i učio hrvatski.
lines.en: Yesterday I worked, and in the evening I watched a film and studied Croatian.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odlično! Kakav film si gledao?
lines.en: Excellent! What kind of film did you watch?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hm... samo malo. Kako se kaže 'comedy' na hrvatskom?
lines.en: Hm... just a moment. How do you say 'comedy' in Croatian?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Komedija!
lines.en: Comedy!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da, gledao sam komediju. Bila je jako smiješna.
lines.en: Yes, I watched a comedy. It was very funny.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A što radiš danas poslije našeg razgovora?
lines.en: And what are you doing today after our chat?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Danas idem u trgovinu, a onda ću kuhati večeru.
lines.en: Today I'm going to the store, and then I'll cook dinner.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Što ćeš kuhati?
lines.en: What will you cook?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nisam siguran... možda rižoto. Volim kuhati, ali nisam odličan kuhar.
lines.en: I'm not sure... maybe risotto. I like cooking, but I'm not an excellent cook.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ha! A koji su ti planovi za vikend?
lines.en: Ha! And what are your plans for the weekend?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Za vikend ću ići na izlet s prijateljima. Ako bude sunčano, idemo na jezero.
lines.en: At the weekend I'll go on a trip with friends. If it's sunny, we're going to the lake.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Zvuči super. Vidiš — pravi razgovor, sve na hrvatskom!
lines.en: Sounds great. See — a real conversation, all in Croatian!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Stvarno? Super! Hvala ti na razgovoru.
lines.en: Really? Great! Thanks for the conversation.

activities.title: Lifeline drill
activities.intro: Seven quick questions on the phrases that keep a conversation alive.

questions.prompt: You didn't hear what a stranger said. What do you say?
questions.explanation: A stranger gets Vi: možete + molim Vas. 'Možeš li ponoviti?' is the friend version.

questions.prompt: Ask for a word you don't know: 'Kako se ___ "beach" na hrvatskom?'
questions.explanation: Kako se kaže...? = How does one say...? (Answer, by the way: plaža.)

questions.prompt: What does 'Što znači ta riječ?' ask for?
questions.explanation: Što znači...? = What does ... mean? Use kako se kaže...? when you need the Croatian word.

questions.prompt: A female speaker says 'I'm not sure': 'Nisam ___.'
questions.explanation: The adjective agrees with the speaker: siguran (m) → sigurna (f).

questions.prompt: Ask them to slow down (politely): 'Speak more slowly, please.'
questions.explanation: Govorite (formal imperative) + sporije (comparative) + molim Vas.

questions.prompt: Your partner tells you surprising good news. The most natural reaction:
questions.explanation: Stvarno? Super! = Really? Great! Reactions keep you an active partner in the chat.

questions.prompt: Turn the question back to a friend in two words: '___ ___?' (And you?)
questions.explanation: A ti? — the tiniest, hardest-working sentence in Croatian conversation. Formal: A Vi?

days.title: Reading a real text
days.objective: Read an authentic short article, menu or post.
days.paretoFocus: Authentic material proves your skills transfer beyond textbooks and exposes real usage.

activities.title: A real text: reading a rental ad
activities.intro: Today you read authentic-style Croatian — a rental ad, the kind you'd see on Njuškalo or a noticeboard. Don't translate word by word: first scan for what, where, how much, when. Then study each line below.

items.hr: OGLAS: Iznajmljuje se mali stan u centru Zagreba.
items.en: AD: A small apartment in the centre of Zagreb is for rent.
items.note: iznajmljuje se = 'is being rented out'. The se-construction is everywhere in ads: prodaje se (for sale), traži se (wanted).

items.hr: Stan ima jednu sobu, kuhinju i kupaonicu.
items.en: The apartment has one room, a kitchen and a bathroom.
items.note: imati + accusative list: soba → sobu, kuhinja → kuhinju, kupaonica → kupaonicu.

items.hr: Blizu su tramvaj, trgovina i park.
items.en: A tram, a shop and a park are nearby.
items.note: blizu = near/nearby. Plural subject → su. Word order is flexible: 'Blizu su...' fronts the key selling point.

items.hr: Cijena je 450 eura mjesečno, plus režije.
items.en: The price is 450 euros per month, plus utilities.
items.note: režije = utility bills — THE word to know in any Croatian rental. mjesečno = monthly.

items.en: The apartment is available from 1 September.
items.note: slobodan = free/available; od + genitive for dates. rujan = September (Croatian months are Slavic, not Latin!).
nazovite = formal imperative of nazvati (to call); poslije + genitive (sati). Croatia uses the 24-hour clock.

activities.title: Sign language: key phrases from real-world texts
activities.intro: These words appear on shop doors, ads, posters and websites all over Croatia. Recognising them instantly is what makes 'authentic' texts readable.

items.note: The se-forms of iznajmiti (rent out) and prodati (sell) — headline words of every ad.
AKCIJA! on a shop window = sale. na akciji = on sale.
Days abbreviate: pon, uto, sri, čet, pet, sub, ned. h = sati.
The pair on every shop door. Zatvoreno nedjeljom = closed on Sundays.
Not to be confused with slobodno (free = available/unoccupied).
Header of official notes on doors and noticeboards — worth reading when you see it.
In ads: hitno prodajem = urgent sale. From hitan = urgent.
Also: ulaz slobodan = free entry. On motorways izlaz = motorway exit.

activities.title: Did you really read it?
activities.intro: Comprehension check on the rental ad plus the real-world phrases. Scan back to the text if needed — that's what real readers do.

questions.prompt: What is being offered in the ad?
questions.explanation: Iznajmljuje se mali stan = a small apartment is for rent.

questions.prompt: Koliko soba ima stan?
questions.explanation: Stan ima jednu sobu, kuhinju i kupaonicu — one room plus kitchen and bathroom.

questions.prompt: What must you pay on top of the 450 euros? 'plus ___' (utilities)
questions.explanation: režije = utility bills (electricity, water, heating). Always ask about them when renting in Croatia!

questions.prompt: From when is the apartment available?
questions.explanation: od 1. rujna = from 1 September. rujan is September — Croatian month names are Slavic.

questions.prompt: When should you call the number in the ad?
questions.explanation: poslije 17 sati = after 17:00, i.e. after 5 p.m. Croatia uses the 24-hour clock.

questions.prompt: A shop window shouts 'sale / special offer' in one word: '___!'
questions.explanation: AKCIJA! = special offer/sale. na akciji = on sale.

questions.prompt: 'Radno vrijeme: pon – pet 8 – 20 h' means the shop is...
questions.explanation: pon–pet = ponedjeljak to petak (Mon–Fri); radno vrijeme = opening hours.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between 'besplatno' and 'slobodno'?
options: besplatno = free of charge; slobodno = free/available
questions.answer: besplatno = free of charge; slobodno = free/available
questions.explanation: besplatan = costing nothing; slobodan = unoccupied/available (like the apartment: slobodan od 1. rujna).

days.title: Writing day
days.objective: Write a 150-word text about your life and plans.
days.paretoFocus: Writing forces precision in cases, aspect and spelling and consolidates everything.

items: Note the tricky phrases for tomorrow.

activities.title: Building a personal text: opening, body, closing
activities.intro: Today you write 150 words about your life and plans. Good A1 texts are built, not improvised: a greeting, a body that mixes tenses and connectors, and a proper sign-off. Steal these building blocks.

items.note: draga for a woman, dragi for a man — adjective agreement even in greetings.

items.hr: Poštovani gospodine Horvat, / Poštovana gospođo Kovač,
items.en: Dear Mr Horvat, / Dear Mrs Kovač, (formal opening)
items.note: Yesterday's register rules apply on paper too: formal reader → Poštovani/Poštovana.

items.hr: Zovem se Ana i živim u Zagrebu već dvije godine.
items.en: My name is Ana and I've been living in Zagreb for two years.
items.note: Classic first body sentence: name + where you live. već + time = 'already/for' with the present tense.

items.hr: Trenutno radim kao učitelj i učim hrvatski.
items.en: Currently I work as a teacher and I'm learning Croatian.
items.note: trenutno = currently; raditi kao + nominative = to work as.
Sequencers give your paragraph a spine — examiners and readers love them.

items.hr: Prošle godine sam bio u Splitu, a sljedeće godine ću putovati u Dubrovnik.
items.en: Last year I was in Split, and next year I'll travel to Dubrovnik.
items.note: One sentence, two tenses — mixing perfekt and futur is exactly what a strong A1 text does. (Woman: bila.)

items.hr: Volim more i sunce, ali ne volim gužvu.
items.en: I love the sea and the sun, but I don't like crowds.
items.note: Opinions give the text personality; gužva (crowd/traffic jam) is a very Croatian complaint.
Linkers that upgrade a list of sentences into a real paragraph.

items.en: See you soon! Write to me! (informal closing)
items.note: piši = informal imperative of pisati; mi = dative 'to me'.
Lijep pozdrav is the everyday sign-off — often shortened to LP in messages.

activities.title: Assemble the text
activities.intro: Before you write your own 150 words, prove you can assemble the skeleton: openers, connectors, tense mixing and sign-offs.

questions.prompt: You are writing to your friend Ivana. The right opening is:
questions.explanation: A female friend gets draga (feminine) — informal register for a friend.

questions.prompt: Say what you do now: '___ radim kao konobar.' (Currently)
questions.explanation: trenutno = currently/at the moment — the classic opener for the 'now' part of your text.

questions.prompt: Which set are the sequencers, in the right order?
questions.explanation: prvo (first) → onda (then) → zatim (after that) → na kraju (finally). The third option are connectors, not sequencers.

questions.prompt: Build the two-tense sentence: 'Last year I was in Split.' (male speaker)
questions.explanation: Prošle godine = time phrase first, then clitic sam in second position, then bio u Splitu (locative).

questions.prompt: Link two ideas: 'Volim more, ___ ne volim gužvu.' (but)
questions.explanation: ali = but. i/ali/jer/pa are the four connectors that carry an A1 text.

questions.prompt: The plan for next year — which sentence is correct?
options: Sljedeće godine ću putovati u Dubrovnik.
Sljedeće godine ću putovao u Dubrovnik.
Sljedeće godine sam putovati u Dubrovnik.
questions.answer: Sljedeće godine ću putovati u Dubrovnik.
questions.explanation: Future = ću + INFINITIVE (putovati). ću + participle or sam + infinitive are impossible combinations.

questions.prompt: Sign off a friendly message: 'Lijep ___!' (Best regards)
questions.explanation: Lijep pozdrav — the everyday closing, shortened to LP in texting.

questions.prompt: Order the closing line: 'Write to me soon!'
questions.explanation: Piši (imperative) + mi (dative clitic 'to me', second position) + uskoro (soon).

days.title: Full cheatsheet review
days.objective: Re-derive the entire one-page summary from memory.
days.paretoFocus: Reproducing the core map proves the 20% is internalised, not just recognised.
drills: Cover the cheatsheet; rebuild each section.
Conjugate biti + one verb in each tense.

items: Check your reconstruction against the app.

activities.title: The whole cheatsheet in 10 lines
activities.intro: Your task today: rebuild the one-page summary from memory. Here is the entire A1 core compressed to ten lines. Read each, close your eyes, and re-derive the full rule before checking the note.

items.en: 30 letters, 30 sounds — read exactly as written!
items.note: Perfectly phonetic. Specials: č/ć = ch, š = sh, ž = zh, đ/dž = j, lj = ly, nj = ny, c = ts, j = y.
Gender shows in the ending: consonant = masculine, -a = feminine, -o/-e = neuter. And there are NO articles.

items.hr: sam, si, je, smo, ste, su — nisam, nisi, nije...
items.en: am, are, is, are, are, are — am not, aren't, isn't...
items.note: biti short forms carry every sentence; the negative fuses into one word.

items.hr: radim, radiš, radi, radimo, radite, rade
items.en: I work, you work, he works, we work, you work, they work
items.note: Present endings -m, -š, –, -mo, -te, -e/-ju. The ending shows the subject, so pronouns are optional.
Nominative does the action, accusative receives it. Feminine -a → -u; masculine inanimate doesn't change.
Aspect: imperfective = the movie, perfective = the snapshot of it finished. Prefixes often make perfectives.

items.hr: Radio sam. / Radila sam. / Radili smo.
items.en: I worked (m). / I worked (f). / We worked.
items.note: Perfekt = biti + participle; the participle agrees in gender and number: -o/-la/-lo/-li/-le.

items.en: Tomorrow I will work. I won't work.
items.note: Futur = ću/ćeš/će/ćemo/ćete/će + infinitive; negative fuses to neću.

items.hr: tko, što, gdje, kada, zašto, kako, koliko + li
items.en: who, what, where, when, why, how, how much + yes/no 'li'
items.note: Seven question words open every question; 'li' after the verb makes yes/no questions: Govoriš li engleski?

items.hr: i, ali, jer, pa, onda — malo ljepila, velike rečenice
items.en: and, but, because, so, then — a little glue, big sentences
items.note: Connectors turn phrases into discourse. Add molim/hvala/oprostite and you're never impolite.

activities.title: Cheatsheet sweep: all 10 sections
activities.intro: One question per cheatsheet section, roughly in rising difficulty. Any miss = re-read that section tonight.

questions.prompt: Which letter makes the 'sh' sound in Croatian?
questions.explanation: š = sh (škola), č = ch (čaj), ž = zh (žena), c = ts (cipela).

questions.prompt: What gender is 'more' (sea)?
questions.explanation: -o/-e endings signal neuter: more, mlijeko, selo.

questions.prompt: Negative of 'je': 'Ona ___ ovdje.' (She is not here.)
questions.explanation: biti negatives are one word: nisam, nisi, nije, nismo, niste, nisu.

questions.prompt: Conjugate: 'Mi ___ svaki dan.' (raditi — we work)
questions.explanation: mi → -mo: radimo. The ending alone tells you the subject is 'we'.

questions.prompt: In 'Marko čita knjigu', which word is in the accusative?
questions.explanation: knjiga → knjigu: the object of čita. Marko is nominative (the subject).

questions.prompt: 'Popio sam kavu.' The verb popiti tells you the action was...
questions.explanation: popiti is perfective — the snapshot of a finished action. piti would be the ongoing process.

questions.prompt: A woman says 'I was at home': '___ sam kod kuće.'
questions.explanation: Participle of biti agrees with gender: bio (m) → bila (f).

questions.prompt: Make it future: 'I will study Croatian.'
questions.explanation: Futur = ću + infinitive. Also possible without the pronoun: Učit ću hrvatski.

questions.prompt: Ask the yes/no question: 'Do you speak English?'
questions.explanation: Yes/no questions: verb first, then li — Govoriš li engleski?

questions.prompt: Give the reason: 'Učim hrvatski ___ volim Hrvatsku.' (because)
questions.explanation: jer = because — the connector that turns statements into explanations.

days.title: Feynman day: teach it all back
days.objective: Explain Croatian's core systems as if teaching a friend.
days.paretoFocus: Teaching is the strongest test of understanding; gaps surface immediately and get fixed.
drills: Complete all Croatian Feynman concepts in the app.
Re-teach any you can't explain cleanly.

activities.title: Teach it back: one-line explanations
activities.intro: Feynman day. If you can explain a rule to a friend in one plain sentence, you own it. Each item asks the 'why' question a beginner would ask — say your answer OUT LOUD before reading the note, then compare.

items.hr: Zašto 'kavu', a ne 'kava'? — Jer je kava objekt.
items.en: Why 'kavu' and not 'kava'? — Because the coffee is the object.
items.note: Teach it: cases are name tags showing each word's JOB. That's why Croatian word order can be free — the tag travels with the word.

items.en: Why 'Radila sam'? — Because a woman is speaking.
items.note: Teach it: the past participle is half-adjective — it agrees with the subject: radio/radila/radili.

items.en: Why is there no 'the'? — Croatian has no articles.
items.note: Teach it: 'žena' covers woman / a woman / the woman; context does the work articles do in English.

items.en: Why 'Govorim' without 'ja'? — The ending -m already means 'I'.
items.note: Teach it: every present-tense ending is a built-in pronoun (-m = I, -š = you...). Saying ja twice is saying 'I I speak'.

items.hr: Zašto 'popiti', a ne 'piti'? — Jer je radnja završena.
items.en: Why 'popiti' and not 'piti'? — Because the action is finished.
items.note: Teach it: imperfective is the movie, perfective is the photo of the finish line. One meaning, two verbs.

items.hr: Zašto 'u Zagrebu', ali 'u Zagreb'? — Lokacija ili smjer.
items.en: Why 'u Zagrebu' but 'u Zagreb'? — Location versus direction.
items.note: Teach it: same preposition, different case. Locative = where you ARE; accusative = where you're GOING.

items.hr: Zašto 'sam' uvijek na drugom mjestu? — Klitike vole drugo mjesto.
items.en: Why is 'sam' always in second position? — Clitics love second position.
items.note: Teach it: sam/si/je are unstressed little words that lean on the first word of the sentence: Jučer SAM radio.

items.hr: Zašto 'Nemam ništa'? — Hrvatski traži dvostruku negaciju.
items.en: Why 'Nemam ništa'? — Croatian requires double negation.
items.note: Teach it: unlike English, ni-words (ništa, nitko, nikad) MUST pair with a negated verb. Two negatives = one strong no.

activities.title: The 'why' test
activities.intro: Not 'what is correct' but 'WHY is it correct'. If you can answer these, you can teach Croatian's core — which means you've learned it.

questions.prompt: Why can Croatian move words around ('Kavu pijem' = 'Pijem kavu') without losing the meaning?
options: case endings show each word's role
questions.answer: case endings show each word's role
questions.explanation: The -u on kavu marks it as object wherever it stands. Cases are name tags for jobs.

questions.prompt: Why does a woman say 'bila sam' while a man says 'bio sam'?
options: the past participle agrees with the subject's gender
questions.answer: the past participle agrees with the subject's gender
questions.explanation: The participle behaves like an adjective: -o (m), -la (f), -li/-le (pl).

questions.prompt: Why is 'Ja govorim hrvatski' usually shortened to 'Govorim hrvatski'?
options: the -m ending already identifies the subject as 'I'
questions.answer: the -m ending already identifies the subject as 'I'
questions.explanation: Verb endings are built-in pronouns. Ja is kept only for emphasis or contrast.

questions.prompt: Complete the Feynman answer: 'Idem u Zagreb' uses the accusative because it expresses ___ (direction/smjer), not location.
questions.explanation: u + accusative = motion towards (u Zagreb); u + locative = static location (u Zagrebu).

questions.prompt: Why does Croatian have BOTH 'pisati' and 'napisati' for 'to write'?
options: one shows the process, the other a completed action
questions.answer: one shows the process, the other a completed action
questions.explanation: Aspect pairs: imperfective pisati = the movie; perfective napisati = the finished snapshot.

questions.prompt: 'Jučer sam gledao film.' Why can't the sentence start 'Sam jučer...'?
options: sam is a clitic and must stand in second position
questions.answer: sam is a clitic and must stand in second position
questions.explanation: Clitics (sam, si, je, mi, te...) are unstressed and lean on the first element — they can never open a sentence.

questions.prompt: Explain 'Ne znam ništa': Croatian requires ___ negation (one word, English).
questions.explanation: Ni-words must co-occur with a negated verb: ne znam ništa, nikad ne radim, nitko nije došao.

questions.prompt: A friend asks: 'Is Croatian spelling hard?' The correct one-line answer:
options: No — every letter is always pronounced the same way, so spelling equals sound
Yes — many letters are silent like in French
Yes — the same letter changes sound depending on the word
No — because there are only 20 letters
questions.answer: No — every letter is always pronounced the same way, so spelling equals sound
questions.explanation: 30 letters, 30 sounds, zero silent letters. If you can say it, you can spell it.

days.title: Mock A1 review
days.objective: Simulate an A1-style test across all four skills.
days.paretoFocus: A timed, four-skill mock reveals exactly where you stand and what to prioritise next.
drills: Do listening, reading, writing and a speaking task.
Take both A0 and A1 quizzes in the app.

items: Clear your due words in the Words tab.

activities.title: Exam Croatian: the instructions you'll meet
activities.intro: Before the mock, learn the meta-language: real Croatian tests are written IN Croatian. These are the instruction lines you will see on any A1 exam paper — never lose points for not understanding the question itself.

items.note: pročitati = perfective of čitati — read it through to the end. Formal imperative in -te.
odgovoriti NA + accusative — answering happens 'onto' the question in Croatian.
dopuniti = to fill in / complete. Your FILL exercises, in exam language.
točan = correct; netočan = incorrect. Multiple choice, Croatian style.
The classic reading-comprehension format on A1/A2 papers.
poslušati = perfective of slušati. The listening-section opener.
riječi = genitive plural of riječ (word) after a number — the writing task.

items.en: Match the words with the pictures.
items.note: povezati = to connect; sa slikama = instrumental plural. Common in vocabulary sections.

activities.title: Mini mock A1: everything, mixed
activities.intro: Ten questions sweeping the whole 60 days — alphabet to futur, café to clinic — in rising difficulty. Treat it like the real thing: no notes, no cheatsheet. Score yourself honestly and review every miss.

questions.prompt: How is 'č' pronounced?
questions.explanation: č = hard ch. ž = s in pleasure, c = ts, j = y. Day 1 knowledge — forever useful.

questions.prompt: A stranger greets you at noon. You reply:
questions.explanation: Midday + stranger = Dobar dan. Dobro jutro is for the morning; Bok, stari! is very informal.

questions.prompt: Introduce yourself: 'Ja ___ studentica iz Kanade.' (biti)
questions.explanation: ja → sam. And note the correct 'iz Kanade' — origin with iz + genitive.

questions.prompt: 'On ___ u restoranu.' (raditi — he works)
questions.explanation: 3rd person singular has no ending: on radi. -m = ja, -e = oni, -te = vi.

questions.prompt: Order at the café: 'Molim Vas jednu ___ i čašu vode.' (kava)
questions.explanation: Object → accusative: kava → kavu. Bonus: čašu vode = glass OF water, genitive.

questions.prompt: Ask where the station is:
questions.explanation: Question word + verb + subject: Gdje je kolodvor? Survival Croatian in three words.

questions.prompt: A woman says 'I didn't work yesterday':
questions.explanation: Negative perfekt = nisam + participle, agreeing with gender: radila. nisam replaces sam directly.

questions.prompt: Make a plan: 'Sutra ___ ići na more.' (I will)
questions.explanation: Futur: ću + infinitive (ići). Negative would be: Sutra neću ići.

questions.prompt: Full A1 sentence: 'Yesterday I bought a ticket and travelled to Split.' (male speaker)
questions.explanation: Clitic sam second, two participles share it (kupio... putovao), u + accusative for direction.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is completely correct?
options: Živim u Zagrebu, nemam auta i svaki dan idem na posao tramvajem.
Živim u Zagreb, ne imam auto i svaki dan idem u posao s tramvaj.
Živim na Zagrebu, nemam auto i svaki dan idem na posao na tramvaju.
Živim u Zagrebu, ne imam auta i svaki dan idem u posao tramvajem.
questions.answer: Živim u Zagrebu, nemam auta i svaki dan idem na posao tramvajem.
questions.explanation: u Zagrebu (locative), nemam + genitive (auta), na posao (na-word), tramvajem (instrumental of transport). Every trap from day 53, dodged.

days.title: Milestone: solid A1 + your A2 roadmap
days.objective: Confirm A1 mastery and plan the path to A2.
days.paretoFocus: Closing the sprint with a clear next plan turns a 60-day burst into a lasting habit.
drills: Your A2 path is ready-made: finish the remaining A1.hr units, then start a2.ffzg.unizg.hr at 1-2 units a day.

items: Tick off every A1 can-do statement.
Commit to daily Words reviews + a weekly tutor session going forward.
Celebrate — you reached A1 in 60 days!

activities.title: Your A1 can-do list and the road to A2
activities.intro: Sixty days ago you couldn't read 'čaj'. Read each can-do statement aloud — in Croatian, naturally — and honestly tick it off. Then look at the roadmap: A2 is not a new mountain, just the next stretch of the same path.

items.hr: Mogu se predstaviti i reći odakle sam.
items.en: I can introduce myself and say where I'm from.
items.note: Day 3 material — now automatic. Ja sam... iz... Drago mi je!

items.hr: Mogu naručiti hranu i piće i pitati koliko što košta.
items.en: I can order food and drink and ask how much things cost.
items.note: Days 18-19: Molim Vas jednu kavu... Koliko košta? Accusative in the wild.

items.hr: Mogu govoriti o sadašnjosti, prošlosti i budućnosti.
items.en: I can talk about the present, the past and the future.
items.note: Prezent + perfekt + futur = the complete A1 tense toolkit. Radim, radio sam, radit ću.

items.hr: Mogu pitati za smjer, kupiti kartu i snaći se na putu.
items.en: I can ask for directions, buy a ticket and get by while travelling.
items.note: Gdje je...? Kartu za Split, molim. snaći se = to manage/find one's way — a very Croatian skill.

items.hr: Mogu pročitati oglas, jelovnik i kratku poruku.
items.en: I can read an ad, a menu and a short message.
items.note: Day 55 proved it: authentic texts are yours now, with se-forms, režije and radno vrijeme.

items.hr: Mogu napisati kratki tekst o sebi i svojim planovima.
items.en: I can write a short text about myself and my plans.
items.note: Day 56: opening, body with connectors and mixed tenses, closing. Lijep pozdrav!

items.hr: Sljedeći korak: A2 — svi padeži u praksi i glagolski vid.
items.en: Next step: A2 — all the cases in practice, plus verb aspect.
items.note: You've MET genitive, dative, locative, instrumental and vocative. A2 makes them automatic and adds aspect pairs in bulk.

items.hr: Plan za A2: završite A1.hr, zatim a2.ffzg.unizg.hr — 1-2 lekcije dnevno.
items.en: A2 plan: finish A1.hr, then a2.ffzg.unizg.hr — 1-2 units a day.
items.note: Same free University of Zagreb e-course, next level. Your routine already exists; only the content changes.

items.hr: Navika pobjeđuje motivaciju: 15 minuta ponavljanja svaki dan.
items.en: Habit beats motivation: 15 minutes of review every day.
items.note: Daily Words-tab reviews + one weekly conversation keep everything you built from fading.

activities.title: Day 60: the celebration conversation
activities.intro: The final script — a celebratory chat looking back on the whole journey. Read it aloud with feeling, then live it: have your real 15-minute celebration conversation with a partner or tutor. Živjeli!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Ili... bok! Danas slavimo — šezdeset dana hrvatskog!
lines.en: Good day! Or... hi! Today we celebrate — sixty days of Croatian!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bok! Ne mogu vjerovati. Prije šezdeset dana nisam znao ni jednu riječ.
lines.en: Hi! I can't believe it. Sixty days ago I didn't know a single word.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A sada? Što sve možeš?
lines.en: And now? What can you do?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mogu se predstaviti, naručiti kavu, kupiti kartu i pričati o svom danu.
lines.en: I can introduce myself, order a coffee, buy a ticket and talk about my day.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Sjećaš se prvog dana? Abeceda: č, ć, š, ž...
lines.en: Remember day one? The alphabet: č, ć, š, ž...

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da! Prvi tjedan je bio težak, ali sada čitam sve — kako piše, tako čitam.
lines.en: Yes! The first week was hard, but now I read everything — as it's written, so I read it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Što je bilo najteže?
lines.en: What was the hardest part?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Padeži, naravno! Ali sada znam: pijem kavu, ne 'pijem kava'.
lines.en: The cases, of course! But now I know: 'pijem kavu', not 'pijem kava'.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! A što ti je bilo najdraže?
lines.en: Bravo! And what did you enjoy most?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Razgovori kao ovaj. Prije dva mjeseca sam samo slušao — danas govorim!
lines.en: Conversations like this one. Two months ago I only listened — today I speak!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: I što ćeš raditi sljedeće?
lines.en: And what will you do next?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nastavit ću učiti. Prvo ću završiti A1 lekcije, a onda počinjem A2.
lines.en: I'll keep learning. First I'll finish the A1 units, and then I'm starting A2.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Odličan plan. Kako se osjećaš danas?
lines.en: An excellent plan. How do you feel today?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Osjećam se ponosno. Hvala ti na svemu — učio sam svaki dan i isplatilo se.
lines.en: I feel proud. Thank you for everything — I studied every day and it paid off.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Nema na čemu. Živjeli — i vidimo se na putu prema A2!
lines.en: You're welcome. Cheers — and see you on the road to A2!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Živjeli! Doviđenja... to jest — vidimo se!
lines.en: Cheers! Goodbye... that is — see you!

activities.title: Victory lap
activities.intro: Seven final questions — one from each corner of your 60 days. Not a test any more; just proof of how far you've come.

questions.prompt: Day 1 flashback: how many letters does the Croatian alphabet have?
questions.explanation: 30 letters, 30 sounds, zero exceptions — the foundation everything else stood on.

questions.prompt: The first phrase of your journey — 'thank you': '___'
questions.explanation: hvala — you've now said it roughly six hundred times. It never stops being useful.

questions.prompt: Which toast will you use to celebrate today?
questions.explanation: Živjeli! = Cheers! (literally 'may we live long'). The others are goodbye, sorry, and here-you-go.

questions.prompt: Your proudest correction: 'Pijem ___, ne "pijem kava".'
questions.explanation: The accusative -u: the single ending that unlocked real Croatian sentences.

questions.prompt: Your journey in one past-tense sentence: 'I studied Croatian every day.' (male speaker)
questions.explanation: Perfekt with the clitic sam in second position — a woman would say: Učila sam hrvatski svaki dan.

questions.prompt: Your promise in one future sentence: 'Nastavit ___ učiti.' (I will continue)
questions.explanation: Nastavit ću učiti — the futur carries you to A2. When ću follows the infinitive, the infinitive drops its final -i.

questions.prompt: The A2 roadmap says your next daily resource is:
options: starting a new language from zero
questions.explanation: The free University of Zagreb A2 e-course continues exactly where A1.hr ends. Habit beats motivation — vidimo se na A2!
